# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Question 1]** 
> **Q1.** Define psychrotrophic bacteria and give two examples. Why are they significant in chilled food storage?
> 
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 
> **Definition (1 mark):** Psychrotrophic (or psychrotolerant) bacteria are organisms that can grow at refrigeration temperatures (≤ 7°C) although their optimum growth temperature lies in the mesophilic range of 20–30°C. They are distinct from true psychrophiles, which have an optimum below 15°C.
> 
> **Examples (1 mark):** *Pseudomonas fluorescens*, *Pseudomonas fragi*, *Listeria monocytogenes*, *Brochothrix thermosphacta*, *Shewanella putrefaciens*.
> 
> **Significance (1 mark):** They are the principal cause of spoilage in chilled foods (meat, fish, poultry, milk), where they grow slowly at 4°C and produce off-odors, slime, and pigments. *Listeria monocytogenes* is also a psychrotrophic pathogen, posing a serious health risk in RTE foods.

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Question 2]** 
> **Q2.** Differentiate between thermoduric and thermophilic bacteria with suitable examples.
> 
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 
> | Basis | Thermoduric (1.5 marks) | Thermophilic (1.5 marks) |
> |:--|:--|:--|
> | Definition | Survive high temperatures but do not grow | Grow actively at high temperatures |
> | Temperature behavior | Survive pasteurization; grow in mesophilic range | Optimum growth 55–75°C |
> | Examples | *Bacillus subtilis*, *Clostridium botulinum* | *Geobacillus stearothermophilus*, *Bacillus coagulans* |
> | Food significance | Survive pasteurization in milk and contaminate products | Cause flat-sour spoilage in canned foods stored hot |

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Question A]**
> 
> **Q3A.** 
> **(a)** [7 marks] Classify the major groups of bacteria associated with food spoilage based on their temperature and pH tolerance. Discuss the growth characteristics, key examples, and the type of foods in which each group is significant.
> 
> **(b)** [7 marks] Explain the term "thermoduric bacteria." Discuss the mechanism of heat resistance with reference to spore-formers, and describe their significance in pasteurized milk.

### Model Solution for Q3A

#### Solution to (a) — 7 marks

> [!NOTE]
> A complete answer for a 7-mark KTU question on this topic must include a tabular classification (3 marks), growth characteristics (2 marks), and food-significance + examples (2 marks).

**Classification of Food Spoilage Bacteria (3 marks):**

| Group | Cardinal T (°C) | pH range | Mechanism of Resistance | Examples |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| Psychrotrophs | T_min -5, T_opt 25, T_max 35 | 4.0 – 9.0 | Cold-active enzymes, unsaturated fatty acids | *Pseudomonas*, *Listeria* |
| Mesophiles | T_min 10, T_opt 37, T_max 45 | 4.5 – 9.0 | Standard metabolism | Most spoilage bacteria |
| Thermophiles | T_min 35, T_opt 65, T_max 80 | 4.5 – 8.0 | Heat-stable enzymes, modified membranes | *G. stearothermophilus* |
| Thermodurics | Survive 80°C, grow at 20–40 | 4.5 – 9.0 | Spore formation, thick cell wall | *Bacillus* spp., *Clostridium* spp. |
| Acidurics | T_opt 25–35, T_min 0–5 | 1.5 – 6.0 | Membrane ATPases, GAD, urease | *Alicyclobacillus*, *Acetobacter* |

**Growth Characteristics (2 marks):**
- Psychrotrophs double in 6–8 h at 4°C, producing lipases, proteases, and pigmented colonies.
- Thermophiles double in 20–30 min at 65°C but are dormant at refrigeration.
- Acidurics can grow at pH 2.5–4.0, exploiting niches where most competitors are inhibited.
- Thermodurics are characterized by **survival** rather than active growth at high temperatures; their vegetative growth resumes at moderate temperatures.

**Food Significance and Examples (2 marks):**
- *Psychrotrophs*: Spoilage of chilled meat, fish, milk (Pseudomonas putrefaction; *Listeria* safety risk).
- *Thermophiles*: Flat-sour spoilage in canned vegetables, evaporated milk, sugar refineries.
- *Acidurics*: Spoilage of apple/orange juice by *Alicyclobacillus* (guaiacol taint), vinegar spoilage by *Acetobacter*.
- *Thermodurics*: Post-pasteurization spoilage of milk (Bacillus, Micrococcus), canned food spoilage (Clostridium).

#### Solution to (b) — 7 marks

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners award marks for: definition (1), spore mechanism (3), significance in pasteurized milk (2), and prevention (1).

**Definition (1 mark):**
Thermoduric bacteria are those that survive pasteurization temperatures (63°C/30 min or 72°C/15 s) but do not necessarily grow at those temperatures. They are characterized by the ability of their vegetative cells or spores to withstand thermal processing.

**Mechanism of Heat Resistance in Spore-Formers (3 marks):**

The bacterial endospore is a metabolically dormant, highly dehydrated structure with the following resistance mechanisms:

1. **Core dehydration:** The protoplast of the mature spore contains only 10–25% water (vs. 80% in vegetative cells). Water removal inhibits both enzyme activity and protein denaturation. This is achieved through the accumulation of **calcium-dipicolinate (Ca-DPA)** in the spore core, which replaces water molecules.

2. **Spore coats and cortex:** A multilayered protein coat and a thick peptidoglycan cortex protect against chemical and enzymatic assault. The cortex maintains the dehydrated state of the core.

3. **DNA protection:** Small acid-soluble spore proteins (SASPs) bind to DNA and protect it from heat-induced depurination, hydrolysis, and oxidative damage. They also serve as a source of energy during germination.

4. **Modified membrane lipids:** Spore inner membrane lipids are largely immobile at high temperatures due to high proportions of saturated fatty acids, preventing heat-induced leakage.

5. **Repair enzymes:** Spores contain a high level of dipicolinic acid and the enzyme catalase, which neutralize reactive oxygen species upon germination.

**Significance in Pasteurized Milk (2 marks):**
- Thermoduric spore-formers (*Bacillus subtilis*, *B. licheniformis*, *B. cereus*, *Geobacillus stearothermophilus*) and non-spore-formers (*Micrococcus*, *Enterococcus*) survive HTST or LTLT pasteurization.
- Post-pasteurization contamination by these organisms leads to:
  - **Sweet curdling** (*B. cereus* — proteolytic enzymes curdle milk without acid).
  - **Ropiness** (*Alcaligenes viscolactis* — EPS production).
  - **Off-flavors** (lipase and protease activity during refrigerated storage).
  - **Bitty cream defect** (lipase action on fat globule membrane).
- Source: udder surface, milking equipment, soil, feed, and biofilm in processing lines.

**Prevention (1 mark):**
- Aseptic packaging post-pasteurization.
- Strict sanitation of milking and processing equipment.
- Use of bacteriocins or thermization (sub-pasteurization pre-treatment).
- Cold storage of finished product to suppress spore-germination and outgrowth.

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Question B]**
> 
> **Q3B.** 
> **(a)** [7 marks] Discuss in detail the microflora associated with fresh meat, poultry, fish, milk, and fruits & vegetables. Highlight the principal spoilage organisms in each category.
> 
> **(b)** [7 marks] Describe the spoilage of canned foods by thermophilic bacteria, with specific reference to flat-sour spoilage and "sulphide-stinker" spoilage. Mention the preventive measures employed in the canning industry.

### Model Solution for Q3B

#### Solution to (a) — 7 marks

> [!NOTE]
> A 7-mark KTU answer on microflora of food groups should be organized by food group, with dominant flora, source, and one characteristic spoilage organism per group. Plan for ~1.4 marks per food group.

**Fresh Meat and Poultry (1.5 marks):**
- **Source:** Animal skin, hide, intestinal tract, slaughterhouse knives, water, hands.
- **Initial flora (pre-chilling):** Mesophilic Enterobacteriaceae, *Streptococcus*, *Staphylococcus*, *Lactobacillus*, *Brochothrix thermosphacta*.
- **Dominant spoilage flora (chilled):** Psychrotrophic Gram-negative rods — *Pseudomonas* spp. (*P. fragi*, *P. fluorescens*, *P. putida*), *Moraxella*, *Acinetobacter*, *Aeromonas*, *Shewanella putrefaciens*.
- **Spoilage characteristics:** Slime on surface, greenish/brown discoloration, putrid odor (H₂S, methyl mercaptan, putrescine, cadaverine).

**Fresh Fish (1.5 marks):**
- **Source:** Skin, gills, intestinal tract, slime layer, ice, seawater, handling surfaces.
- **Initial flora:** *Pseudomonas*, *Moraxella*, *Acinetobacter*, *Vibrio*, *Aeromonas*, *Photobacterium*, *Shewanella putrefaciens*.
- **Dominant spoilage flora:** *Shewanella putrefaciens* (the most prolific H₂S producer in temperate-water fish), *Pseudomonas*.
- **Spoilage characteristics:** Trimethylamine (TMA) production from TMAO, off-odor, slime.

**Milk (1.5 marks):**
- **Source:** Udder (interior and exterior), milking equipment, air, water, feed, soil, handlers.
- **Initial flora:** *Streptococcus* (*S. agalactiae*, *S. uberis*), *Lactobacillus*, coliforms, *Staphylococcus*, *Micrococcus*, *Bacillus* (from soil).
- **Dominant spoilage flora (pasteurized):** Psychrotrophic *Pseudomonas*, *Bacillus* spp., coliforms.
- **Spoilage characteristics:** Souring (lactic acid from LAB), ropiness (alkaligenes, *Micrococcus*), proteolysis, lipolysis, malty flavor (*Streptococcus lactis* var. *maltigenes*).

**Fruits and Vegetables (1.5 marks):**
- **Source:** Soil, air, water, insects, post-harvest handling, damaged tissue.
- **Initial flora:** *Erwinia*, *Pseudomonas*, *Xanthomonas*, *Corynebacterium*, *Lactobacillus*, yeasts (*Candida*, *Pichia*), molds (*Botrytis*, *Rhizopus*, *Alternaria*, *Aspergillus*).
- **Dominant spoilage flora:** *Erwinia carotovora* (soft rot), *Botrytis cinerea* (gray mold), *Rhizopus stolonifer* (bread mold), *Penicillium expansum* (blue mold of apples).
- **Spoilage characteristics:** Soft rot, mushy texture, mold growth, fermentation, off-flavor.

**Cereals and Grains (1 mark, bonus):**
- *Bacillus subtilis*, *B. cereus*, *Aspergillus flavus* (aflatoxin), *Fusarium* spp.

#### Solution to (b) — 7 marks

> [!NOTE]
> A 7-mark KTU answer on thermophilic canned-food spoilage must cover: definition + groups (2 marks), flat-sour mechanism + example (2 marks), sulphide-stinker mechanism + example (2 marks), and prevention (1 mark).

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Thermophilic bacteria in canned foods have an optimum growth temperature of 55–75°C. They cause two classical types of spoilage — *flat-sour* and *sulphide-stinker* — both characterized by intact can seams (no gas pressure buildup) and an off-odor/off-flavor.

**Flat-Sour Spoilage (2.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** Spoilage with no can bulging (flat can) but with souring due to acid production.
- **Causative organism:** *Geobacillus stearothermophilus* (formerly *Bacillus stearothermophilus*) — a Gram-positive, spore-forming, facultative anaerobe.
- **Optimum growth:** 55–65°C, with growth range 30–75°C.
- **Mechanism:** The organism ferments carbohydrates (sugars in the canned product) to organic acids (lactic, acetic, formic) without producing gas, hence the *flat* can. Lactate lowers pH to 4.5–4.8.
- **Affected products:** Low-acid canned vegetables (corn, peas, spinach, asparagus), evaporated milk, cream, sugar-beet juice.
- **Detection:** pH drop (from 6.0 to 4.5), characteristic "cheesy" sour odor, Gram-positive spore-formers on microscopic examination.

**Sulphide-Stinker Spoilage (2.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** Spoilage where the can interior turns black and emits a strong rotten-egg odor due to H₂S production.
- **Causative organism:** *Clostridium nigrificans* (renamed *C. thermopalmarium*) — an anaerobic, thermophilic, H₂S-producing spore-former.
- **Optimum growth:** 55°C.
- **Mechanism:** Reduces the sulphur-containing amino acids (cysteine, methionine) or sulphite to H₂S. The H₂S reacts with iron from the can wall to form **ferrous sulphide (FeS)**, a black precipitate that discolors the can interior and the food.
- **Affected products:** Canned corn, peas, and other vegetables rich in protein.
- **Detection:** Black discoloration of can interior, H₂S odor, low spore load before processing.

**Preventive Measures in the Canning Industry (1 mark):**
1. Store canned foods at temperatures **below 30°C** (preferably below 20°C) to suppress thermophilic outgrowth.
2. Avoid slow cooling of cans after retort processing (rapid cooling through 55–30°C).
3. Use **single-strength** rather than concentrated sugar and starch ingredients (less thermal mass).
4. Pre-blanch vegetables at 90°C for 5–10 min to reduce thermophilic spore load.
5. Maintain hygiene of can seamers and processing lines.
6. Conduct **pre-incubation** testing of canned lots at 55°C for 7 days to detect flat-sour and sulphide-stinker potential.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse thermoduric with thermophile.** Students frequently write "thermophilic bacteria survive pasteurization." This is INCORRECT. Thermophiles *grow* at 55–75°C; thermodurics *survive* pasteurization but grow at mesophilic temperatures.
> 2. **Do not write "psychrophile" for "psychrotroph"** in chilled-food questions. Psychrophiles are *rare in food*; the dominant spoilage group is psychrotrophs.
> 3. **Do not state that all aciduric bacteria are beneficial (vinegar producers).** Mention *Alicyclobacillus acidoterrestris* as the key *spoilage* aciduric.
> 4. **In flat-sour questions, always state the can remains flat** (no gas) — failing to mention this loses 1 mark.
> 5. **In sulphide-stinker questions, mention FeS (black precipitate)** and the reaction with H₂S — vague answers lose 1 mark.
> 6. **Always provide temperature ranges (T_min, T_opt, T_max)** when asked to "define" a group — verbal definitions alone fetch only partial credit.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 ESE Revision — 60-Second Scan Points:**

- [x] **Psychrotrophs** → grow at ≤ 7°C, optimum 20–30°C, cause spoilage of chilled meat/fish/milk. Examples: *P. fluorescens*, *L. monocytogenes*, *B. thermosphacta*, *Shewanella*.
- [x] **Psychrophiles** → optimum ≤ 15°C, not significant in food spoilage. Common exam distractor.
- [x] **Thermodurics** → *survive* pasteurization; key organisms are spore-formers (*B. subtilis*, *B. cereus*, *C. botulinum*, *G. stearothermophilus*). Significant in pasteurized milk.
- [x] **Thermophiles** → *grow* at 55–75°C; cause flat-sour (*G. stearothermophilus*) and sulphide-stinker (*C. nigrificans*) spoilage in canned foods. **Storage below 30°C is the key control.**
- [x] **Acidurics** → grow at pH < 4.5; *Alicyclobacillus acidoterrestris* is the most important spoilage aciduric (guaiacol taint in fruit juices). Heat + acid = control strategy.
- [x] **D-value** = time to kill 90% of population at temperature $T$. **Z-value** = temperature increase to reduce D by 90%. **$F_0$** = equivalent sterilization time at 121.1°C.
- [x] **Arrhenius growth equation** $\mu = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ governs temperature dependence.
- [x] **Food-group microflora quick memory map:** *Cereals → Bacillus*; *Meat → Pseudomonas*; *Fish → Shewanella*; *Milk → Lactococcus, Pseudomonas*; *Eggs → Pseudomonas (green rot)*; *Fruits/veg → Erwinia, Botrytis*; *Sugar → Saccharomyces rouxii*.
- [x] **Five signs of microbial spoilage:** Gas, slime, pigment, off-odor, textural breakdown.
- [x] **Flat-sour vs. sulphide-stinker:** Flat = no gas, *G. stearothermophilus*; Sulphide = H₂S + black FeS, *C. nigrificans*.
- [x] **Hurdle technology** = combination of multiple sub-lethal stresses; multiplicative effect on growth inhibition.
- [x] **Pasteurization standards:** LTLT = 63°C/30 min; HTST = 72°C/15 s; both designed to kill *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* and most non-spore pathogens.
- [x] **Commercial sterilization:** 12D reduction of *C. botulinum* type A = $F_0 = 2.52$ min (industry uses 3 min for safety margin).
- [x] **Memorize the cardinal temperatures table** (psychrophile → hyperthermophile) — a 3-mark definition question is virtually guaranteed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristic Features and Significance of Spoilage of Different Groups of Foods - Cereals, Fruits & Vegetables, Meat, Sea Foods, Eggs, Milk and Milk Products; Sources of Contamination; Fermented Food Products - Prebiotics, Probiotics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT303 - FOOD MICROBIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Characteristic Features and Significance of Spoilage of Different Groups of Foods - Cereals, Fruits & Vegetables, Meat, Sea Foods, Eggs, Milk and Milk Products; Sources of Contamination; Fermented Food Products - Prebiotics, Probiotics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Food Spoilage — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Food Spoilage** is defined as any change in the **visual, textural, olfactory, or nutritional** attributes of a food product that renders it unacceptable for human consumption. Microbiologically, it is the **undesirable metabolic activity of microorganisms** (bacteria, yeasts, moulds) leading to the production of off-flavours, off-odours, gas, slime, discolouration, and/or toxins.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, spoilage is studied under the broader umbrella of **Food Quality Assurance**, where the **Course Outcome (CO3)** emphasizes the student's ability to *identify spoilage-causing microflora, predict shelf-life behaviour, and recommend preservation interventions.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Biological Neighbourhood" Analogy

Imagine a food product as a **city apartment complex**. The nutrients (proteins, carbohydrates, fats, water) are the apartments. Microorganisms are the tenants looking for accommodation.

- **Fresh food** = A secure building with strict access control (intact cell walls, immune barriers, low pH) — tenants cannot enter.
- **Spoiled food** = Broken security (damaged cuticle, pH shift, temperature abuse) — tenants break in, throw wild parties (fermentation), trash the place (gas, slime, off-odour), and eventually the building collapses (complete putrefaction).

The **type of tenant** that moves in depends on the **food's intrinsic properties** (pH, water activity, nutrient profile) and **extrinsic factors** (temperature, oxygen, storage).

### 1.3 The Intrinsic & Extrinsic Parameter Framework

> [!NOTE]
> Every food spoilage study in KTU Module 3 must be interpreted through **two parameter sets**:

| Parameter Set | Variables | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Intrinsic** | pH, Water Activity ($a_w$), Nutrient composition, Antimicrobial constituents, Redox potential ($E_h$) | Built into the food; determines *which* microbes can colonize |
| **Extrinsic** | Storage temperature, Relative Humidity (RH), Atmospheric composition (O₂/CO₂) | Environmental; determines *how fast* spoilage proceeds |

### 1.4 The Key Physico-Chemical Constant: Water Activity ($a_w$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Water Activity ($a_w$)** is the most critical intrinsic parameter governing microbial spoilage. It is the **ratio of the partial vapour pressure of water in the food ($p$) to the vapour pressure of pure water ($p_0$)** at the same temperature.

$$a_w = \frac{p}{p_0}$$

- Most bacteria require $a_w \geq 0.91$
- Most yeasts require $a_w \geq 0.87$
- Most moulds require $a_w \geq 0.70$
- **Halophilic bacteria** (e.g., *Vibrio*) can grow at $a_w$ as low as 0.75
- **Xerophilic moulds** (e.g., *Eurotium*) can grow at $a_w \approx 0.65$

### 1.5 Module 3 Topic Roadmap

This module is structured into **three thematic clusters**:

1. **Cluster A — Spoilage of Individual Food Groups** (Cereals, Fruits & Vegetables, Meat, Sea Foods, Eggs, Milk & Milk Products)
2. **Cluster B — Sources of Contamination** (Primary, secondary, cross-contamination pathways)
3. **Cluster C — Fermented Food Products** (Prebiotics & Probiotics)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Spoilage of Cereals and Cereal Products

### 2.1.1 Characteristic Features

Cereals (rice, wheat, maize, oats, barley) are **low-moisture, low-pH (5.5–6.5) substrates** with $a_w$ in the range **0.60–0.85** when properly stored. Spoilage is therefore dominated by **xerophilic moulds and field fungi**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bacterial spoilage of sound cereals is rare** because the low $a_w$ excludes most bacteria. The spoilage flora is overwhelmingly **mycological** (fungal).

| Spoilage Type | Causative Agent | Characteristic Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Field mould** | *Fusarium*, *Alternaria*, *Cladosporium*, *Helminthosporium* | Discolouration, "black point" in wheat kernels, reduced germination |
| **Storage mould** | *Aspergillus*, *Penicillium*, *Eurotium* (*Asp. glaucus* group) | Musty odour, discolouration, visible mycelium, **"hot spots"** due to respiratory heat |
| **Yeast spoilage** | *Candida*, *Hansenula* | Souring, off-odour in moist cereals |
| **Bacterial spoilage** | *Bacillus* spp., *Clostridium* spp. (in moist grain) | Souring, gas, rope in bread (Bacillus) |

### 2.1.2 Significance of Cereal Spoilage

- **Mycotoxin production** (aflatoxin B₁, ochratoxin A, fumonisin, deoxynivalenol) — carcinogenic, hepatotoxic, nephrotoxic
- **Economic loss** — FAO estimates **5–10% global post-harvest loss** in cereals due to moulds
- **Quality loss** — reduced milling yield, off-flavour (rancidity), discolouration of flour
- **Public health** — aflatoxin contamination in maize/peanuts linked to **hepatocellular carcinoma**

### 2.1.3 Bread Spoilage (Special Case)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Bread spoilage is a **two-phase phenomenon**:
> 1. **Mould spoilage** by *Neoscytalidium*, *Aspergillus niger*, *Penicillium expansum*, *Monilia sitophila* (red bread mould) — visible within **5–7 days** at room temperature.
> 2. **Rope spoilage** by *Bacillus subtilis*, *Bacillus licheniformis*, *Bacillus mesentericus* — produces **ropy, sticky, discoloured crumb** with a fruity/rotten-apple odour. The bacterium produces **capsular polysaccharide** that gives the bread a stringy texture when pulled apart.

## 2.2 Spoilage of Fruits and Vegetables

### 2.2.1 The "Fresh, Intact, Living" Paradigm

> [!NOTE]
> Fruits and vegetables are **living tissues** that continue **respiration, transpiration, and senescence** post-harvest. Spoilage is therefore a complex interaction between **host physiology and microbial invasion**.

**Key characteristic:** Fruits and vegetables are **low-pH, high-water-activity** foods. The pH classification dictates the spoilage flora:

| pH Class | pH Range | Foods | Dominant Spoilage Flora |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Acidic** | < 4.5 | Citrus, apples, berries, pineapple, tomato | **Yeasts** (*Saccharomyces*, *Candida*), **Moulds** (*Penicillium*, *Botrytis*, *Aspergillus*) |
| **Sub-acidic** | 4.5–5.5 | Banana, mango, papaya, cucumber, lettuce | Mixed — **bacteria + yeasts + moulds** |
| **Near-neutral** | > 5.5 | Potato, carrot, onion, artichoke, asparagus | **Bacteria** dominate (*Erwinia*, *Pseudomonas*, *Corynebacterium*) |

### 2.2.2 Major Spoilage Types of Fruits

| Spoilage Type | Causative Agent | Host Foods | Visible Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Soft rot** | *Rhizopus stolonifer*, *Mucor piriformis* | Strawberries, peaches, tomatoes | Rapid liquefaction, fluffy white/black mycelium |
| **Blue mould rot** | *Penicillium italicum*, *P. digitatum* | Citrus fruits | Blue-green powdery sporulation |
| **Green mould rot** | *Penicillium digitatum* | Oranges, lemons | Olive-green spore masses |
| **Grey mould** | *Botrytis cinerea* | Grapes, strawberries, apples | Grey fuzzy mycelium, "noble rot" in winemaking |
| **Brown rot** | *Monilinia fructicola* | Stone fruits (peach, plum) | Brown sunken lesions, concentric rings |
| **Anthracnose** | *Colletotrichum* spp. | Banana, mango, papaya | Dark sunken spots with pink spore masses |
| **Bacterial soft rot** | *Erwinia carotovora* (*Pectobacterium*) | Potato, carrot, onion, lettuce | Mushy, watery, foul-smelling tissue |

### 2.2.3 Significance of Fruit & Vegetable Spoilage

- **High post-harvest losses** — 20–50% in developing countries
- **Food safety risk** — *Salmonella*, *E. coli* O157:H7, *Listeria monocytogenes* linked to fresh produce outbreaks
- **Economic** — international trade restrictions, quarantine issues
- **Nutritional** — vitamin C loss, antioxidant degradation

## 2.3 Spoilage of Meat and Meat Products

### 2.3.1 Meat as a Spoilage Substrate

Meat is a **near-neutral pH (5.5–6.5), high $a_w$ (>0.98), nutrient-rich** food — the **ideal substrate** for bacterial proliferation. Spoilage is **overwhelmingly bacterial**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The post-mortem pH drop is critical.** In a healthy animal, muscle pH drops from ~7.0 to **5.4–5.7** within 24 hours due to **glycogen → lactic acid** conversion. In **PSE (Pale, Soft, Exudative)** meat (stressed animals), the pH drops too rapidly while the carcass is still hot, causing protein denaturation and water loss — making it *more* susceptible to spoilage.

### 2.3.2 The Three Phases of Meat Spoilage

| Phase | Timeframe | Dominant Flora | Substrate → Product | Sensory Defect |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Phase 1 — Glycolytic** | 0–24 h | Endogenous enzymes (no microbes yet) | Glycogen → Lactic acid | Rigor mortis, pH fall |
| **Phase 2 — Proteolytic** | Days 2–6 | *Pseudomonas* spp., *Acinetobacter*, *Moraxella* (aerobic psychrotrophs) | Proteins → Peptides, amino acids | Off-odour, surface slime |
| **Phase 3 — Aminolytic** | Day 6+ | *Brevibacterium*, *Micrococcus*, *Staphylococcus*, *Enterobacteriaceae* | Amino acids → Amines, H₂S, indole, skatole | Putrefaction, H₂S "rotten egg" smell, greening |

### 2.3.3 Characteristic Spoilage Defects of Meat

| Defect | Causative Agent | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Surface slime** | *Pseudomonas*, *Acinetobacter*, *Lactobacillus* (in vacuum-packed) | Growth at meat surface under high RH |
| **Greening (core)** | *Leuconostoc* spp. (heterofermentative) | H₂O₂ production → oxidizes myoglobin to **choleglobin** (green) |
| **Bone taint** | *Clostridium* spp. (anaerobic, deep tissue) | Proteolysis + H₂S production near bone |
| **Souring** | Lactic acid bacteria | Lactic + acetic acid accumulation |
| **Putrefaction** | Mixed anaerobes (*Clostridium*, *Bacteroides*) | Protein → indole, skatole, putrescine, cadaverine |

### 2.3.4 Significance

- **Public health** — Meat is the principal vehicle for *Salmonella*, *Campylobacter jejuni*, *Clostridium perfringens*, *Listeria monocytogenes*, *Yersinia enterocolitica*
- **Economic** — Single largest food category by value lost to spoilage
- **Shelf-life** — Vacuum-packed meat: 14–28 days; aerobic: 3–5 days (chilled)

## 2.4 Spoilage of Sea Foods

### 2.4.1 Marine Microbial Profile

Sea foods are unique because their **initial microbial load is dominated by psychrotrophic, halophilic marine bacteria**. Fish muscle has a low glycogen reserve, so pH drops only to **6.0–6.4** post-mortem — higher than mammalian meat.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Group I pseudomonads** (*Pseudomonas* spp.) and **Shewanella putrefaciens** (formerly *Alteromonas putrefaciens*) are the **primary spoilers of chilled fresh fish**.

### 2.4.2 Characteristic Spoilage Pattern of Fish

| Defect | Causative Agent | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Trimethylamine (TMA) production** | *Shewanella putrefaciens*, *Photobacterium phosphoreum* | Reduces **TMAO (trimethylamine N-oxide)** to TMA via **TMAO reductase**. TMA has a characteristic "fishy" odour. |
| **H₂S production** | *Shewanella*, *Clostridium* | Cysteine desulfhydrase → H₂S (rotten egg) |
| **Inosine → hypoxanthine** | Autolytic + bacterial enzymes | ATP → ADP → AMP → IMP → Inosine → **Hypoxanthine** (bitter taste) — key indicator of fish freshness (K-value) |
| **Sliminess** | *Pseudomonas*, *Aeromonas*, *Vibrio* | Exopolysaccharide production on gills and skin |
| **Histamine (scombroid poisoning)** | *Morganella morganii*, *Klebsiella pneumoniae*, *Proteus* | Histidine → Histamine via **histidine decarboxylase** — high in tuna, mackerel, mahi-mahi |

### 2.4.3 Shellfish Spoilage

Shellfish (oysters, mussels, clams) are **filter feeders** and accumulate **Vibrio spp.** (*V. parahaemolyticus*, *V. vulnificus*) and **norovirus** from seawater. They pose a **dual spoilage + safety risk**.

### 2.4.4 Significance

- **Scombroid poisoning** — histamine intoxication (allergy-like symptoms)
- **Ciguatera** — reef fish accumulate ciguatoxin from dinoflagellates
- **Anisakiasis** — *Anisakis* larvae in raw/undercooked fish
- **High perishability** — shelf-life often **< 2 days** in ice

## 2.5 Spoilage of Eggs

### 2.5.1 The Natural Defence System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A freshly laid egg has **three natural antimicrobial barriers**:
> 1. **Shell + Cuticle** — physical barrier; cuticle is a proteinaceous layer that seals shell pores
> 2. **Shell membranes** — two membranes (inner & outer) provide chemical (lysozyme) and physical barriers
> 3. **Albumen** — high pH (9.0–9.5), contains **avidin** (binds biotin), **conalbumin/ovotransferrin** (chelates iron), and **lysozyme** (hydrolyses peptidoglycan)

As the egg ages, **CO₂ escapes** through the shell pores → albumen pH rises to **9.5–9.7** → vitelline membrane weakens → yolk flattens → **Haugh unit** decreases (freshness indicator).

### 2.5.2 Spoilage Types

| Spoilage Type | Causative Agent | Defect Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Bacterial rots** | *Pseudomonas*, *Proteus*, *Serratia*, *Salmonella* | Black rot, green rot, red rot — coloured rots from pigmented bacteria |
| **Mould rots** | *Penicillium*, *Cladosporium*, *Mucor*, *Aspergillus* | Pinpoint mould spots, "mustiness" |
| **Mixed rots** | Multiple psychrotrophs | General liquefaction, foul odour |
| **Infertility/dead-in-shell** | *Salmonella enteritidis* | Embryonic mortality — significant in breeding flocks |

### 2.5.3 Significance

- *Salmonella enteritidis* colonizes the **ovarian tissue of hens** — transovarian transmission makes eggs a major source of foodborne salmonellosis
- Albumen proteins are **allergenic** (egg allergy is among the top 5 food allergies in children)

## 2.6 Spoilage of Milk and Milk Products

### 2.6.1 Milk as a Microbial Substrate

Milk is the **"perfect food"** — and unfortunately, also the **"perfect microbial growth medium"**:
- pH: 6.6–6.8 (near neutral)
- $a_w$: ~0.997
- Rich in **lactose, casein, whey proteins, lipids, vitamins**
- Contains **lactenins** (initial antimicrobial, lost within 1–2 hours of milking)

### 2.6.2 Types of Milk Spoilage

| Spoilage Type | Causative Agent | Substrate → Product | Sensory Defect |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Souring (acid)** | *Lactococcus lactis*, *Streptococcus thermophilus*, *Lactobacillus* spp. | Lactose → Lactic acid | Sour taste, coagulation at pH ~4.6 (isoelectric point of casein) |
| **Ropiness** | *Alcaligenes viscolactis*, *Enterobacter aerogenes* | Polysaccharide capsule formation | Slimy, stringy milk |
| **Sweet curdling** | *Bacillus subtilis*, *Bacillus cereus* | **Rennin-like protease** at neutral pH (no acid) | Curdles without souring |
| **Bitter flavour** | *Streptococcus liquefaciens* | Proteolysis → bitter peptides | Bitter taste |
| **Malty flavour** | *Streptococcus lactis* var. *maltigenes* | Leucine → 3-methylbutanal (isovaleraldehyde) | Malty, caramel-like |
| **Gas formation** | *Clostridium butyricum*, *Coliforms* | Lactose → CO₂ + H₂ | Foamy, blown cans, floating curds |
| **Red milk** | *Serratia marcescens*, *Brevibacterium erythrogenes* | Pigment (prodigiosin / carotenoid) | Red surface or throughout |
| **Blue milk** | *Pseudomonas syncyanea* | Blue pigment | Blue discolouration |

### 2.6.3 Pasteurization-Resistant Spoilage

> [!NOTE]
> **Psychrotrophic spoilage flora** of refrigerated pasteurized milk:
> - *Pseudomonas fluorescens*, *Ps. fragi*, *Ps. putida*
> - *Achromobacter*, *Alcaligenes*, *Flavobacterium*
> These produce **heat-resistant extracellular proteases and lipases** that survive pasteurization and cause off-flavours in UHT milk even months later.

### 2.6.4 Significance of Milk Spoilage

- Public health: *Mycobacterium bovis* (TB), *Brucella abortus* (undulant fever), *Listeria monocytogenes*, *Coxiella burnetii* (Q-fever)
- Economic: Largest dairy industry loss
- Quality: Off-flavours, reduced cheese yield, faulty fermentation

## 2.7 Sources of Contamination

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Sources are classified as **Primary (direct)**, **Secondary (indirect)**, and **Cross-contamination**.

### 2.7.1 Primary Sources

| Source | Examples | Food Risk |
|---|---|---|
| **Soil** | *Clostridium botulinum*, *Bacillus cereus*, *Listeria monocytogenes* | Root vegetables, grains |
| **Water** | *Vibrio cholerae*, *Coliforms*, *Norovirus* | Shellfish, fresh produce, ice |
| **Air** | *Bacillus*, *Clostridium* (spores), *Penicillium*, *Aspergillus* (spores) | Cereals, milk, cheese |
| **Animals (sick/healthy)** | *Salmonella*, *Campylobacter*, *Brucella* | Meat, milk, eggs |
| **Insects & pests** | *Salmonella*, *Shigella*, *Staphylococcus* (cockroach) | All foods |
| **Humans (handlers)** | *Staph. aureus*, *Streptococcus*, *Hepatitis A*, *Norovirus* | Ready-to-eat foods |

### 2.7.2 Secondary / Cross-Contamination Sources

- **Equipment** — slicers, cutting boards, tanks (biofilms)
- **Processing surfaces** — drains, floors, walls
- **Packaging material** — contaminated cans, bottles
- **Storage** — improper segregation (raw above cooked)

> [!NOTE]
> **Biofilms** are the most insidious secondary source. They are structured microbial communities embedded in an **extracellular polymeric substance (EPS)** matrix, highly resistant to sanitizers. *Listeria monocytogenes* and *Pseudomonas* biofilms in dairy plants are a recurrent KTU exam theme.

## 2.8 Fermented Food Products

### 2.8.1 Prebiotics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Prebiotics** are **non-digestible food ingredients (typically oligosaccharides or dietary fibres)** that **selectively stimulate the growth and/or activity of beneficial bacteria (probiotics) in the colon**, thereby improving host health.

**Famous examples:**
- **Fructo-oligosaccharides (FOS)** — inulin from chicory, garlic, onion, banana
- **Galacto-oligosaccharides (GOS)** — from lactose, in human breast milk
- **Inulin** — chicory root, Jerusalem artichoke
- **Resistant starch** — cooked-and-cooled potato, green banana
- **Lactulose** — synthetic disaccharide

**Mechanism:** Prebiotics are not digested in the upper GI tract (humans lack the enzymes) → reach the colon intact → serve as **selective substrate for *Bifidobacterium* and *Lactobacillus*** → increase SCFA (short-chain fatty acids: acetate, propionate, butyrate) production.

### 2.8.2 Probiotics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Probiotics** are **live microorganisms which, when administered in adequate amounts, confer a health benefit on the host** (FAO/WHO 2002 definition).

**Key genera:**
- *Lactobacillus* — *L. acidophilus*, *L. casei*, *L. rhamnosus*, *L. plantarum*, *L. delbrueckii* subsp. *bulgaricus*
- *Bifidobacterium* — *B. bifidum*, *B. longum*, *B. infantis*, *B. breve*
- *Saccharomyces* — *S. boulardii* (yeast)
- *Enterococcus* — *E. faecium*
- *Streptococcus* — *S. thermophilus*

**Health claims (evidence-supported):**
- Diarrhea prevention/treatment (especially antibiotic-associated)
- Irritable Bowel Syndrome (IBS) symptom reduction
- Lactose intolerance improvement (β-galactosidase activity)
- Modulation of gut immunity
- *Helicobacter pylori* eradication adjunct
- Atopic eczema reduction in infants

### 2.8.3 Synbiotics

> [!NOTE]
> **Synbiotics** = **Probiotic + Prebiotic** combined in one product, designed to **synergistically enhance survival and implantation** of the probiotic in the gut.

Examples: Yogurt + inulin, fermented milk + FOS.

### 2.8.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Definition / Parameter | Key Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Water Activity | $a_w = p / p_0$ | Dimensionless, range 0–1 |
| Bacterial growth limit | $a_w \geq 0.91$ | Most spoilage bacteria |
| Mould growth limit | $a_w \geq 0.70$ | Xerophiles down to 0.65 |
| Cereal $a_w$ (safe storage) | 0.60–0.65 | Below mould growth threshold |
| Fresh meat pH (post-rigor) | 5.4–5.7 | Healthy meat |
| PSE meat pH | < 5.4 | Stressed-animal meat |
| Fresh fish pH (post-mortem) | 6.0–6.4 | Higher than mammalian meat |
| Fresh egg albumen pH | 9.0–9.7 | Increases with age |
| Fresh milk pH | 6.6–6.8 | Neutral |
| Acid-coagulation pH (casein) | 4.6 | Isoelectric point of casein |
| TMAO → TMA (fish) | $TMAO + 2H \rightarrow TMA + H_2O$ | TMAO reductase |
| Histamine (scombroid) | > 50 mg/100 g fish | Toxic threshold |
| Probiotic dose | $\geq 10^6$ CFU/g or per mL | FAO/WHO minimum at consumption |

### 2.8.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

This knowledge underpins the design of:
- **HACCP (Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points)** systems in food processing plants
- **Shelf-life prediction models** (e.g., the *Schoolfield* temperature-kinetics model)
- **Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP)** for fresh produce and meat
- **Synbiotic functional food** development (a booming commercial sector in India and globally)

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Spoilage Decision Tree — Analytical Framework

The analytical process for identifying spoilage in any food is a **systematic decision tree** based on intrinsic and extrinsic parameters. Below is the exhaustive, step-by-step logical derivation:

**Step 1 — Identify the food's $a_w$:**
$$a_w = \frac{p_{food}}{p_{water}}$$
- If $a_w < 0.60$ → Microbial growth impossible (e.g., dried milk powder, biscuits)
- If $0.60 \leq a_w < 0.85$ → Mould spoilage only (e.g., cereals, dried fruits)
- If $0.85 \leq a_w < 0.91$ → Yeasts + moulds (e.g., aged cheese, jam)
- If $a_w \geq 0.91$ → Bacteria, yeasts, moulds (e.g., fresh meat, fish, milk)

**Step 2 — Identify the food's pH:**
- pH < 4.0 → Moulds and yeasts only (e.g., citrus)
- pH 4.0–4.5 → Lactic acid bacteria + yeasts + moulds (e.g., tomato, pineapple)
- pH 4.5–5.5 → Aciduric bacteria (Lactobacillaceae) + yeasts + moulds (e.g., banana, cucumber)
- pH > 5.5 → All bacteria viable (e.g., meat, fish, milk, eggs)

**Step 3 — Identify the dominant nutrient:**
- **High protein** (meat, fish, eggs) → Spoilage flora: *Pseudomonas*, *Shewanella*, *Clostridium*, *Proteus* (proteolytic)
- **High carbohydrate** (cereals, fruits) → Spoilage flora: *Aspergillus*, *Penicillium*, *Saccharomyces*, lactic acid bacteria (glycolytic)
- **High lipid** (butter, ghee) → Spoilage flora: *Achromobacter*, *Pseudomonas*, *Aspergillus*, *Geotrichum* (lipolytic)

**Step 4 — Identify the storage atmosphere:**
- Aerobic storage → *Pseudomonas*, *Aspergillus*, *Penicillium*
- Anaerobic (vacuum/MAP) → *Lactobacillus*, *Clostridium*, *Brochothrix*
- Cold storage (chill 0–4 °C) → *Psychrotrophs* dominate (*Pseudomonas fragi*, *Shewanella*, *Listeria*)
- Frozen storage (-18 °C) → Growth inhibited; spoilage limited to enzymatic + physicochemical (freezer burn, oxidation)

**Step 5 — Synthesize the spoilage prediction profile** based on Steps 1–4.

## 3.2 Detailed Analytical Matrix — Spoilage Characteristics Across Food Groups

| Food Group | Typical pH | Typical $a_w$ | Dominant Nutrient | Primary Spoilage Flora | Signature Spoilage Defect | Microbial Metabolite |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cereals (whole grain)** | 5.5–6.5 | 0.65–0.85 | Starch | *Aspergillus* (esp. *A. flavus*, *A. parasiticus*), *Penicillium*, *Fusarium* | Mustiness, discolouration, hot spots | **Aflatoxin B₁**, ochratoxin A |
| **Bread (bakery)** | 5.0–6.0 | 0.94–0.97 | Starch + protein | *Neoscytalidium*, *Aspergillus niger*, *Penicillium*, *Bacillus subtilis* | Mould bloom, rope | Capsular polysaccharide (rope) |
| **Fruits (acidic)** | 2.5–4.0 | 0.97–0.99 | Sugars | *Penicillium italicum*, *P. digitatum*, *Botrytis cinerea* | Soft rot, blue/green mould | Citric acid consumption, CO₂ |
| **Vegetables (low-acid)** | 5.5–7.0 | 0.97–0.99 | Cellulose + sugars | *Erwinia carotovora* (now *Pectobacterium*), *Pseudomonas*, *Botrytis* | Bacterial soft rot, mushy tissue | **Pectate lyase** → tissue maceration |
| **Meat (fresh)** | 5.4–6.5 | 0.98–0.99 | Protein | *Pseudomonas*, *Acinetobacter*, *Moraxella* (aerobic); *Brochothrix thermosphacta* (anaerobic) | Slime, off-odour, greening | Amines, H₂S, organic acids |
| **Poultry** | 5.7–6.4 | 0.98–0.99 | Protein | *Pseudomonas*, *Acinetobacter*, *Salmonella*, *Campylobacter* | Slime, off-odour, *Campylobacter* gastroenteritis | H₂S, indole |
| **Fish (marine)** | 6.0–6.4 | 0.98–0.99 | Protein + TMAO | *Shewanella putrefaciens*, *Pseudomonas* | TMA (fishy odour), H₂S, histamine | TMA, histamine, hypoxanthine |
| **Shellfish** | 6.2–6.8 | 0.98 | Protein | *Vibrio parahaemolyticus*, *V. vulnificus*, *Pseudomonas* | Rapid putrefaction, paralytic poisoning | **Saxitoxin**, **tetrodotoxin** (from dinoflagellates) |
| **Eggs (whole)** | Albumen 9.0–9.7; yolk 6.0–6.5 | 0.97 | Protein + lipid | *Pseudomonas*, *Proteus*, *Serratia*, *Salmonella enteritidis* | Black/green/red rot, transovarian *Salmonella* | H₂S, pigments |
| **Milk (raw)** | 6.6–6.8 | 0.997 | Lactose + casein | *Lactococcus*, *Lactobacillus*, *Coliforms*, *Pseudomonas* | Souring, ropiness, sweet curdling | Lactic acid, CO₂, capsular EPS |
| **Cheese** | 4.6–6.0 (variable) | 0.95–0.99 (hard to 0.70 aged) | Protein + fat | *Penicillium roqueforti* (blue), *P. camemberti* (white), *Brevibacterium linens* (red smear) | Cheese ripening — desirable vs. over-ripening | Lipases, proteases, ammonia |
| **Butter** | 6.1–6.4 | 0.93–0.95 | Fat | *Pseudomonas fragi*, *Geotrichum candidum*, *Penicillium* | Rancidity, surface taint | Free fatty acids, butyric acid |

## 3.3 The K-Value Derivation for Fish Freshness

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **K-value** is a chemical freshness index for fish, defined as the ratio of the inosine + hypoxanthine fraction to the total ATP breakdown products. It is widely used in Japanese and Asian seafood industry.

**Step 1 — Define the ATP degradation pathway in post-mortem fish:**

$$\text{ATP} \xrightarrow{\text{ATPase}} \text{ADP} \xrightarrow{\text{ADPase}} \text{AMP} \xrightarrow{\text{AMP deaminase}} \text{IMP} \xrightarrow{\text{nucleotidase}} \text{Ino} \xrightarrow{\text{nucleoside phosphorylase}} \text{Hx}$$

**Step 2 — Define K-value:**

$$K = \frac{[\text{Ino}] + [\text{Hx}]}{[\text{ATP}] + [\text{ADP}] + [\text{AMP}] + [\text{IMP}] + [\text{Ino}] + [\text{Hx}]} \times 100\%$$

**Step 3 — Classify fish freshness:**

| K-Value | Freshness Class |
|---|---|
| K < 20% | Sashimi-grade (very fresh) |
| K = 20–40% | Acceptable for raw consumption |
| K = 40–60% | Fresh, but cooking recommended |
| K = 60–80% | Borderline — must be cooked |
| K > 80% | Spoiled |

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation:** The K-value increases with time, temperature, and microbial load. It is a **temperature-time integrated freshness index** and is preferred over simple bacterial counts because it captures the cumulative biochemical deterioration.

## 3.4 The TMAO Reduction Derivation (Fish)

**Step 1 — TMAO's biological role:**
Marine fish use **trimethylamine N-oxide (TMAO)** as an osmolyte and to counteract the destabilizing effect of urea on proteins. TMAO is **odourless**.

**Step 2 — Microbial reduction:**

$$\text{TMAO} + 2H^+ + 2e^- \xrightarrow{\text{TMAO reductase}} \text{TMA} + H_2O$$

**Step 3 — Sensory impact:**
- TMA has a **strong fishy/amine odour** (detection threshold ~0.5 ppm in water)
- TMA is **volatile** — accumulates rapidly in vacuum-packed fish

**Step 4 — Significance:**
- TMA is the **single most reliable chemical index** of chilled marine fish spoilage
- It is used commercially in the **TMA-N value** (TMA-N = TMA × 14/59) for regulatory limits (e.g., < 10 mg TMA-N/100g = fresh)

## 3.5 The Aflatoxin Biosynthesis Pathway (Cereals)

**Step 1 — Causative organisms:** *Aspergillus flavus*, *A. parasiticus*

**Step 2 — Conditions favouring synthesis:**
- $a_w$: 0.83–0.97 (optimum 0.95)
- Temperature: 25–35 °C
- Substrate: maize, peanuts, copra, chillies

**Step 3 — Simplified biosynthesis pathway:**

$$\text{Acetyl-CoA} \rightarrow \text{Polyketide chain (norsolorinic acid)} \rightarrow \text{Averantin} \rightarrow \text{Averufin} \rightarrow \text{Aflatoxin B}_1$$

**Step 4 — Toxicity:**
- **Aflatoxin B₁** is the most potent naturally occurring **hepatocarcinogen** — Group 1 IARC carcinogen
- Activated in liver by **CYP450** to **AFB₁-8,9-epoxide**, which binds guanine in DNA → G → T transversion → p53 mutation (codon 249)
- FSSAI limit: **20 µg/kg** in cereals and nuts; **30 µg/kg** in oilseeds

## 3.6 Probiotic Viability Calculation

**Step 1 — Probiotic dose at point of consumption:**

$$\text{Recommended Dose} \geq 10^6 \text{ CFU/g or per mL at consumption}$$

**Step 2 — Account for die-off during storage (assumes first-order kinetics):**

$$N_t = N_0 \cdot e^{-k t}$$

Where:
- $N_t$ = viable count at time $t$ (CFU/g)
- $N_0$ = initial count at manufacture
- $k$ = die-off rate constant (typically 0.05–0.5 per week for refrigerated products)
- $t$ = storage time (weeks)

**Step 3 — Calculation example:**
A yogurt is manufactured with $N_0 = 10^8$ CFU/g. The probiotic *Lactobacillus acidophilus* has $k = 0.1$/week at 4 °C. After **4 weeks** of refrigerated storage:

$$N_4 = 10^8 \cdot e^{-0.1 \times 4} = 10^8 \cdot e^{-0.4}$$

$$N_4 = 10^8 \cdot 0.6703 = 6.70 \times 10^7 \text{ CFU/g}$$

This is still **far above** the $10^6$ CFU/g threshold, so the product retains its probiotic claim.

**Step 4 — Engineering design implication:**
Manufacturers must either (a) over-dose at $N_0$ so that $N_t$ remains above threshold at the **end of shelf-life**, or (b) use **microencapsulation**, **oxygen-impermeable packaging**, and **prebiotic co-formulation (synbiotic)** to reduce $k$.

## 3.7 Synbiotic Synergy Index

**Step 1 — Define the Synbiotic Index (SI):**

$$SI = \frac{\log(N_{\text{synbiotic}}) - \log(N_{\text{probiotic alone}})}{\log(N_{\text{probiotic alone}})}$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation:**
- SI > 0 → prebiotic enhances probiotic survival
- SI = 0 → no effect
- SI < 0 → antagonism (rare)

**Step 3 — Example:** A *Bifidobacterium longum* + FOS formulation has SI = +0.25, indicating a 25% logarithmic improvement over probiotic alone — typical of a well-designed synbiotic.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Water Activity. State the minimum $a_w$ values for the growth of (i) most spoilage bacteria, (ii) most spoilage yeasts, and (iii) most spoilage moulds. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Water activity ($a_w$)** is the ratio of the partial vapour pressure of water in a food ($p$) to the vapour pressure of pure water ($p_0$) at the same temperature.

$$a_w = \frac{p}{p_0}$$

- (i) Most spoilage bacteria: **$a_w \geq 0.91$**
- (ii) Most spoilage yeasts: **$a_w \geq 0.87$**
- (iii) Most spoilage moulds: **$a_w \geq 0.70$**

Water activity is the **most critical intrinsic parameter** governing microbial spoilage, as it determines the availability of water for microbial metabolism. Foods with $a_w < 0.60$ (e.g., biscuits, dried milk powder) are microbiologically stable.

---

### Question 2: Define the term "Probiotic" as per FAO/WHO. List any four commonly used probiotic genera with one example species each. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Probiotics** are **live microorganisms which, when administered in adequate amounts, confer a health benefit on the host** (FAO/WHO 2002).

Four commonly used probiotic genera:

1. **Lactobacillus** — *L. acidophilus*
2. **Bifidobacterium** — *B. bifidum*
3. **Saccharomyces** — *S. boulardii*
4. **Streptococcus** — *S. thermophilus*

**Therapeutic dose:** $\geq 10^6$ CFU/g or per mL at point of consumption.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — ESE Module Internal Choice

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Spoilage of Cereals, Fruits, and Meat

**Q. (a)** Discuss the characteristic features and significance of spoilage of **cereals and cereal products**. Highlight the role of mycotoxins with special reference to aflatoxin B₁. **(7 Marks)**

**Q. (b)** Explain in detail the **three phases of meat spoilage** with the causative microflora, the substrate changes, and the resulting sensory defects in each phase. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Cereal Spoilage

**1. Introduction to cereals as a substrate (1 mark):**
Cereals (rice, wheat, maize, barley, oats) are low-moisture, low-pH (5.5–6.5) foods with water activity ($a_w$) in the range 0.60–0.85 when properly stored. They are dominated by **starch** as the primary nutrient. Spoilage is overwhelmingly **mycological** (fungal) because the low $a_w$ excludes most bacteria.

**2. Types of cereal spoilage flora (2 marks):**

| Category | Genera | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Field fungi** | *Fusarium*, *Alternaria*, *Cladosporium*, *Helminthosporium* | Pre-harvest invasion; cause "black point" in wheat |
| **Storage fungi** | *Aspergillus*, *Penicillium*, *Eurotium* (*A. glaucus* group) | Post-harvest; cause mustiness, hot spots |
| **Yeasts** | *Candida*, *Hansenula* | Spoilage of moist cereals |
| **Bacteria** | *Bacillus*, *Clostridium* (in moist grain) | Souring, rope in bread |

**3. Bread rope spoilage (1 mark):**
Caused by *Bacillus subtilis* and *B. licheniformis* spores that survive baking. They produce a **capsular polysaccharide** (levan) that gives the crumb a **stringy, sticky, discoloured** appearance with a fruity-rotten apple odour.

**4. Aflatoxin B₁ — significance and biosynthesis (2 marks):**
- **Causative organisms:** *Aspergillus flavus*, *A. parasiticus*
- **Conditions:** $a_w$ 0.83–0.97, temperature 25–35 °C, substrates: maize, peanuts, copra
- **Biosynthesis:** Acetyl-CoA → polyketide chain (norsolorinic acid) → averantin → averufin → **Aflatoxin B₁**
- **Toxicity:** Aflatoxin B₁ is a **Group 1 IARC carcinogen** (most potent natural hepatocarcinogen)
- **Mechanism:** Bioactivated in liver by CYP450 to **AFB₁-8,9-epoxide**, which forms an adduct with guanine N7 → G→T transversion → p53 mutation (codon 249) → **hepatocellular carcinoma**
- **FSSAI limit:** 20 µg/kg in cereals and nuts

**5. Significance of cereal spoilage (1 mark):**
- **Health risk** — hepatocellular carcinoma (aflatoxin), nephrotoxicity (ochratoxin A), alimentary toxic aleukia (Fusarium toxins)
- **Economic** — 5–10% global post-harvest loss (FAO estimate)
- **Quality** — reduced milling yield, rancidity, discolouration

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Three Phases of Meat Spoilage

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
Fresh meat is a near-neutral pH (5.4–6.5), high $a_w$ (>0.98), nutrient-rich substrate — **ideal for bacterial growth**. The post-mortem pH drop from 7.0 to 5.4–5.7 is critical: in **PSE meat** (stressed animals), pH falls too rapidly while the carcass is hot, causing protein denaturation and increased spoilage susceptibility.

**2. Phase 1 — Glycolytic (0–24 h) (2 marks):**
- **Flora:** No microbial growth yet; **endogenous enzymes** dominate
- **Substrate change:** Glycogen → Lactic acid (anaerobic glycolysis)
- **Sensory change:** **Rigor mortis**, pH fall, muscle stiffening
- **Significance:** This phase determines the **ultimate pH** of the meat, which influences subsequent microbial colonization

**3. Phase 2 — Proteolytic (Days 2–6) (2 marks):**
- **Flora:** *Pseudomonas* spp. (especially *P. fragi*, *P. fluorescens*), *Acinetobacter*, *Moraxella* (aerobic psychrotrophs); *Brochothrix thermosphacta* in vacuum packs
- **Substrate change:** Proteins → Peptides → Amino acids (via extracellular proteases)
- **Sensory change:** Off-odour (sour, cheesy), surface slime, first signs of discolouration
- **Significance:** This is the **rate-limiting phase** of chilled meat spoilage

**4. Phase 3 — Aminolytic (Day 6+) (2 marks):**
- **Flora:** *Brevibacterium linens*, *Micrococcus*, *Staphylococcus*, *Enterobacteriaceae*, *Clostridium* spp. (in deep tissue)
- **Substrate change:** Amino acids → **Amines** (putrescine, cadaverine), H₂S, indole, skatole, ammonia
- **Sensory change:** Putrefaction, **H₂S rotten-egg smell**, tissue softening, **greening** (choleglobin formation)
- **Significance:** This is the **terminal phase** — meat is unfit for human consumption

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Milk Spoilage, Sea Foods, Prebiotics, and Probiotics

**Q. (a)** Describe the characteristic spoilage types of **milk and milk products** with their causative organisms and biochemical basis. **(7 Marks)**

**Q. (b)** Differentiate between **prebiotics and probiotics** with suitable examples. Explain the concept of **synbiotics** and the mechanism by which prebiotics selectively promote probiotic growth in the colon. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Milk Spoilage Types

**1. Milk as a substrate (1 mark):**
Milk (pH 6.6–6.8, $a_w$ ~0.997, rich in lactose and casein) is the **ideal microbial growth medium**. It contains natural antimicrobials called **lactenins**, which are lost within 1–2 hours of milking.

**2. Spoilage types (5 marks):**

| Spoilage Type | Causative Agent | Biochemical Basis | Sensory Defect |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Acid souring** | *Lactococcus lactis*, *Lactobacillus* spp. | Lactose → Lactic acid (homolactic fermentation) | Sour taste, coagulation at pH 4.6 (casein isoelectric point) |
| **Ropiness** | *Alcaligenes viscolactis*, *Enterobacter aerogenes* | Polysaccharide capsule formation | Slimy, stringy milk |
| **Sweet curdling** | *Bacillus subtilis*, *B. cereus* | Rennin-like protease at neutral pH | Curdles without souring |
| **Bitter flavour** | *Streptococcus liquefaciens* | Proteolysis → bitter peptides | Bitter taste |
| **Malty flavour** | *Streptococcus lactis* var. *maltigenes* | Leucine → 3-methylbutanal (isovaleraldehyde) | Malty, caramel-like |
| **Gas formation** | *Clostridium butyricum*, *Coliforms* | Lactose → CO₂ + H₂ | Foamy, blown cans |
| **Red milk** | *Serratia marcescens* | Prodigiosin pigment | Red discolouration |
| **Blue milk** | *Pseudomonas syncyanea* | Blue pigment | Blue discolouration |

**3. Pasteurization-resistant spoilage (1 mark):**
*Pseudomonas fluorescens*, *Ps. fragi*, and *Achromobacter* produce **heat-resistant extracellular proteases and lipases** that survive pasteurization and UHT processing, causing gelation and off-flavours in stored milk.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Prebiotics vs Probiotics vs Synbiotics

**1. Prebiotics — Definition & Examples (2 marks):**
> [!NOTE]
> **Prebiotics** are **non-digestible food ingredients (typically oligosaccharides or dietary fibres) that selectively stimulate the growth and/or activity of beneficial bacteria in the colon, improving host health.**

**Examples:**
- **Fructo-oligosaccharides (FOS)** — inulin from chicory, garlic, onion, banana
- **Galacto-oligosaccharides (GOS)** — from lactose, present in human breast milk
- **Inulin** — chicory root, Jerusalem artichoke
- **Resistant starch** — cooked-and-cooled potato, green banana
- **Lactulose** — synthetic disaccharide

**2. Probiotics — Definition & Examples (2 marks):**
> [!NOTE]
> **Probiotics** are **live microorganisms which, when administered in adequate amounts, confer a health benefit on the host** (FAO/WHO 2002).

**Examples:**
- *Lactobacillus acidophilus*, *L. casei*, *L. rhamnosus*, *L. plantarum*
- *Bifidobacterium bifidum*, *B. longum*, *B. breve*
- *Saccharomyces boulardii* (yeast)
- *Streptococcus thermophilus*
- **Recommended dose:** $\geq 10^6$ CFU/g or per mL at point of consumption

**3. Synbiotic concept (1 mark):**
> [!NOTE]
> **Synbiotics** = **Probiotic + Prebiotic** combined in a single product, designed to **synergistically enhance survival and implantation** of the probiotic in the gut.

**Examples:** Yogurt + inulin; fermented milk + FOS; *Bifidobacterium* + GOS.

**4. Mechanism of prebiotic action (2 marks):**
1. Prebiotics **resist digestion** in the upper GI tract (humans lack the hydrolytic enzymes for β-(2→1) and β-(2→6) glycosidic bonds of FOS/GOS)
2. They **reach the colon intact** and serve as a **selective substrate** for *Bifidobacterium* and *Lactobacillus*
3. The colonic bacteria ferment the prebiotic to **short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs)**: acetate, propionate, butyrate
4. SCFAs **lower colonic pH**, **inhibit pathogens** (*Salmonella*, *Clostridium*), and **provide energy to colonocytes**
5. Bifidogenic effect also **modulates gut immunity** and increases **mucin production** for gut barrier integrity

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing $a_w$ thresholds** — Students often state mould growth at 0.91 (bacterial threshold). Moulds grow at $a_w \geq 0.70$, not 0.91.
> 2. **Citing only one example of aflatoxin-producing mould** — Mention both *A. flavus* AND *A. parasiticus* for full credit.
> 3. **Missing the pH drop explanation in meat spoilage** — Rigor mortis and PSE meat are part of the answer, not separate.
> 4. **Conflating histamine with TMA in fish** — TMA is from TMAO reduction; histamine is from histidine decarboxylation. They are **two different spoilage pathways**.
> 5. **Omitting dose in probiotic definitions** — The FAO/WHO definition mandates "**in adequate amounts**" — quoting the dose ($\geq 10^6$ CFU/g) earns full credit.
> 6. **Forgetting to mention synbiotic mechanism** — Synbiotics are NOT just mixtures; they are designed to **synergistically enhance survival**.
> 7. **Skipping the engineering/preservation angle** — A complete answer should connect spoilage features to **shelf-life, HACCP, or preservation strategies**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### A. Core Intrinsic Parameters
- **$a_w$** is the most critical single parameter for microbial growth
- Bacterial limit: $a_w \geq 0.91$; Yeast: $\geq 0.87$; Mould: $\geq 0.70$
- pH < 4.5 → Moulds + yeasts only; pH > 5.5 → All bacteria viable

### B. Cereals & Bakery
- Cereals: $a_w$ 0.60–0.85 → dominated by *Aspergillus* and *Penicillium*
- Aflatoxin B₁ (*A. flavus*, *A. parasiticus*) → hepatocarcinogen (Group 1 IARC)
- Bread: Mould spoilage + **rope spoilage** (*Bacillus subtilis* capsular polysaccharide)

### C. Fruits & Vegetables
- Acidic fruits → Mould + yeast spoilage (*Penicillium*, *Botrytis*)
- Low-acid vegetables → **Bacterial soft rot** by *Erwinia carotovora* (pectate lyase)
- Pectate lyase = the **molecular scissor** of vegetable spoilage

### D. Meat
- Three phases: **Glycolytic → Proteolytic → Aminolytic**
- Primary spoilage flora: *Pseudomonas*, *Acinetobacter*, *Moraxella*
- **H₂S, indole, skatole, putrescine, cadaverine** = signature putrefaction volatiles
- *Salmonella*, *Campylobacter*, *Listeria* = safety triad

### E. Sea Foods
- Fish: **TMAO → TMA** (signature of marine fish spoilage, *Shewanella putrefaciens*)
- K-value: ATP breakdown index for fish freshness
- Scombroid poisoning: histamine (from *Morganella morganii*) > 50 mg/100 g

### F. Eggs
- Three barriers: **Shell + Cuticle → Shell Membranes → Albumen (pH 9.0–9.7)**
- Antimicrobials: **Lysozyme, Ovotransferrin, Avidin**
- *Salmonella enteritidis* — transovarian transmission

### G. Milk & Milk Products
- Lactic acid bacteria → acid souring; coagulation at pH 4.6 (casein pI)
- *Bacillus* → sweet curdling; *Alcaligenes* → ropiness
- Heat-resistant proteases from *Pseudomonas* → pasteurization-resistant spoilage

### H. Sources of Contamination
- Primary: Soil, Water, Air, Animals, Humans, Insects
- Secondary: Equipment, **Biofilms**, packaging, storage
- **Biofilms** are the most insidious — *Listeria* and *Pseudomonas* persisters

### I. Prebiotics
- Non-digestible, selectively fermented, host-benefit
- FOS, GOS, Inulin, Resistant starch
- Mechanism: → Colon → Substrate for *Bifidobacterium*/*Lactobacillus* → SCFAs

### J. Probiotics
- Live microbes, adequate dose ($\geq 10^6$ CFU/g), host benefit
- Genera: *Lactobacillus*, *Bifidobacterium*, *Saccharomyces*, *Streptococcus*
- Dose survival: $N_t = N_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$ (first-order die-off)

### K. Synbiotics
- Probiotic + Prebiotic combined
- Synergistic enhancement of probiotic survival and implantation

### L. Numerical & Regulatory Reminders
- Aflatoxin FSSAI limit: **20 µg/kg** in cereals
- Histamine toxic threshold: **50 mg/100 g** fish
- K-value sashimi grade: **< 20%**
- Casein isoelectric point: **pH 4.6**
- Probiotic minimum dose: **$10^6$ CFU/g**
- Egg albumen pH (fresh): **9.0**, rises to **9.7** with age
- Meat ultimate pH (post-rigor): **5.4–5.7**
- Fish post-mortem pH: **6.0–6.4** (higher than mammalian meat)

### M. Quick "Mnemonic Anchor"
- **"BBMP" for spoilage types of milk:** **B**itter (*S. liquefaciens*), **B**lue (*Ps. syncyanea*), **M**alty (*S. lactis* var. *maltigenes*), **P**igmented red (*Serratia*) — and add **R**opiness, **S**our, **S**weet curdling for completeness.
- **"MAP" for meat spoilage phases:** **M**uscle enzymes (Phase 1) → **A**erobic pseudomonads (Phase 2) → **P**utrefactive anaerobes (Phase 3).
- **"GPA" for fish spoilage chemistry:** **G**uanine derivatives (K-value) → **P**utrescine/amines → **A**mine (TMA, histamine).

### N. Common KTU 2024 Scheme Question Patterns
- "Discuss the characteristic features of spoilage of [food]. Name the causative organisms and the biochemical changes involved." (Module 3 staple)
- "Differentiate between prebiotics and probiotics with examples." (Almost guaranteed every year)
- "Explain the role of water activity in food spoilage." (Frequently tested)
- "Discuss the three phases of meat spoilage with the causative microflora." (High-weightage Part B)
- "What are synbiotics? Explain their mechanism with a suitable example." (Recent trend)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spoilage of Fermented Foods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT303 - FOOD MICROBIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Spoilage of Fermented Foods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🧫 SPOILAGE OF FERMENTED FOODS — Section 1: Foundations

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Spoilage of Fermented Foods (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> Spoilage of fermented foods refers to the post-fermentation deterioration of microbiologically transformed food substrates, caused by the metabolic activity of contaminating or surviving spoilage microorganisms (yeasts, molds, and bacteria), leading to unacceptable changes in sensory attributes (flavor, aroma, texture, appearance), nutritional quality, and/or safety of the product.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** framework, fermented food spoilage is examined across three pillars:
1. **Causative Agent** (which microorganism?)
2. **Substrate Susceptibility** (which fermented product?)
3. **Process Failure** (which step broke?)

### Formal Categories of Spoilage Defects

| Defect Class | Sensory Manifestation | Typical pH Window |
|--------------|----------------------|-------------------|
| **Visible Growth** | Mold, yeast films, scum, pink spots | 3.0 – 7.0 |
| **Off-Flavor** | Bitterness, rancidity, "mousy," fruity, yeasty | 3.5 – 6.5 |
| **Texture Loss** | Slime, ropiness, gas holes, syneresis | 4.0 – 6.5 |
| **Color Defect** | Discoloration, browning, bleaching | 3.0 – 7.0 |
| **Gas Defect** | H₂S, CO₂, butyric explosion (late blowing) | 4.5 – 7.0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:**
> A *fermented food* is NOT sterile. It contains a controlled starter population (LAB, yeast, or mold) plus residual contaminants. Spoilage occurs when **non-starter microorganisms overcome the barrier of low pH, low aw, organic acids, and competitive exclusion.**

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Concert Hall" Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **🎻 Real-World Analogy — The Fermentation Orchestra:**
> Think of a fermented food like a **perfectly tuned orchestra** performing in a concert hall. The **starter cultures** (Lactobacillus, Streptococcus, Saccharomyces) are the principal musicians — they have played their part and produced a beautiful product (yogurt, cheese, beer). But the **hall is not locked**. Outside, in the **corridors (the processing environment, air, equipment, raw materials)**, lurk **uninvited musicians** — stray yeasts like *Candida*, molds like *Penicillium*, and rogue bacteria like *Bacillus* or *Clostridium*. These opportunists, when conditions allow (a door left open, i.e., improper salt, pH, temperature, or packaging), sneak in and play their own discordant music — **this is spoilage**. The conductor (the food technologist) must keep the hall secure through **Hurdle Technology**.

### Key Spoilage Categories (Simplified for First Read)

1. **🦠 Microbial Spoilage** — Yeasts, molds, spoilage bacteria, bacteriophages.
2. **⚗️ Chemical/Biochemical Spoilage** — Oxidative rancidity, enzymatic breakdown, non-enzymatic browning.
3. **📦 Physical Spoilage** — Package failure, gas accumulation, moisture migration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Module 3 specifically emphasizes the **microbial dimension** of spoilage, with the chemical/physical aspects acting as supporting knowledge. Always anchor your answer to a **specific microorganism + specific fermented food + specific defect**.

---

## 1.3 The Spoilage Triangle

```
        RAW MATERIAL QUALITY
              /\
             /  \
            /    \
           / SPOI-\
          / LAGE   \
         /__________\
   PROCESS         ENVIRONMENT
   (Salt, pH,      (Air, T, RH,
    T, time)        equipment)
```

Every spoilage event is the **intersection** of these three factors. KTU examiners reward answers that identify all three vertices.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Tables

## 2.1 The Six Pillars of Fermented Food Spoilage (Theory)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Cognitive Cue:** Examiners test your ability to **link** a specific spoilage defect → specific microorganism → specific food. Memorize the following **6 Pillar Mechanism**:

### Pillar 1 — **Surface Mold Growth (Filamentous Fungi)**
- **Genera involved:** *Penicillium*, *Aspergillus*, *Mucor*, *Geotrichum*, *Cladosporium*, *Monilia*, *Alternaria*
- **Mechanism:** Spore germination on oxygen-exposed product surface → mycelial mat → pigmented colonies (white, blue-green, black, pink).
- **Typical pH of growth:** **3.0 – 7.0** (broad range, low pH-tolerant).
- **Affected foods:** Surface-ripened cheeses (Camembert, Brie), sauerkraut, soy sauce, miso, cured meats.
- **KTU Buzzwords:** "White fluffy growth," "blue-green sporulation," "Camembert defect."

### Pillar 2 — **Yeast Spoilage (Film Yeasts & Fermentative Yeasts)**
- **Genera involved:** *Saccharomyces* (wild), *Zygosaccharomyces rouxii*, *Candida*, *Pichia*, *Hansenula*, *Brettanomyces/Dekkera*, *Torulopsis*
- **Mechanism:** Osmotolerant yeasts ferment residual sugars → CO₂ + ethanol → bulging containers; *Brettanomyces* produces **4-ethylphenol** (phenolic, "Band-aid" off-flavor).
- **Affected foods:** Yogurt, sour cream, wine, beer, pickles, vinegar.
- **KTU Buzzwords:** "Gushing," "yeasty off-flavor," "film formation (velum)," "sherry-like aroma."

### Pillar 3 — **Bacterial Spoilage by Non-LAB**
- **Genera involved:** *Bacillus subtilis* (ropy bread), *Clostridium butyricum* (late blowing of cheese), *Clostridium tyrobutyricum*, *Acetobacter* (vinegar spoilage in wine), *Zymomonas* (cider sickness), *Pediococcus* (beer sarcina sickness), *Lactobacillus brevis* (beer/acid rot), *Enterobacter*, *Pseudomonas*.
- **Mechanism:** Heterolactic or butyric fermentation → CO₂ + H₂ + butyric acid → gas defects; proteolysis → bitterness; lipolysis → rancidity.
- **KTU Buzzwords:** "Late blowing," "butyric explosion," "ropiness," "Acetobacter pellicle."

### Pillar 4 — **Bacteriophage Attack**
- **Targets:** Starter LAB cultures (*Streptococcus thermophilus*, *Lactobacillus delbrueckii* subsp. *bulgaricus*, *Lactococcus lactis*).
- **Mechanism:** Viral lysis of starter cells → fermentation fails or stalls → pH does not drop → food becomes vulnerable to pathogens and spoilage organisms.
- **Affected foods:** Cheese, yogurt, fermented milks.
- **KTU Buzzwords:** "Phage failure," "no acid development," "PR phage," "lysogeny."

### Pillar 5 — **Chemical/Enzymatic Defects**
- **Lipolysis** by residual lipases → free fatty acids → **rancidity** (cheese, cured meats).
- **Proteolysis** by residual proteases → peptides → **bitterness** (casein breakdown in cheese).
- **Oxidation** of unsaturated fatty acids → peroxides → **TBA value increase** in fermented meats.
- **Maillard browning** in dried fermented products (soy sauce, miso).

### Pillar 6 — **Physical/Packaging Defects**
- **Syneresis** (whey separation) in yogurt from acid shock or improper stabilizer.
- **Package bulging** from CO₂ accumulation.
- **Surface drying/cracking** in mold-ripened cheeses — premature dehydration.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Spoilage Matrix (Food × Defect × Organism)

> [!TIP]
> This is the **most-asked cross-table** in KTU Module 3 questions. Memorize this matrix — examiners frequently give a product name and ask to identify the spoilage organism, OR vice versa.

| **Fermented Food** | **Typical Spoilage Defect** | **Causative Microorganism** | **Mechanism** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Yogurt** | Surface yeast film, fruity/yeasty flavor | *Candida*, *Saccharomyces*, *Pichia* | Ferment lactose → CO₂ + ethanol |
| **Yogurt** | Mold spots (white, green, black) | *Penicillium*, *Aspergillus*, *Mucor* | Aerobic sporulation on surface |
| **Soft Cheese** | Late blowing, gas holes, butyric odor | *Clostridium tyrobutyricum*, *C. butyricum* | Butyric fermentation of lactate |
| **Hard Cheese** | Pink/brown discoloration | *Brevibacterium linens* (overgrowth), *Serratia marcescens* | Pigment production |
| **Cheddar** | Bitter taste | Residual proteinases + non-starter LAB (*L. casei*, *L. plantarum*) | Excessive proteolysis |
| **Cured Meats (Salami)** | Surface mold, slime | *Penicillium*, *Geotrichum candidum*, *Micrococcaceae* | Aerobic growth on casing |
| **Sauerkraut** | Pink Kraut | *Serratia marcescens* (or yeasts producing red pigment) | Pigment synthesis at low pH |
| **Sauerkraut** | Softening, slime | *Bacillus*, *Aerobacter*, pectinolytic molds | Pectin degradation |
| **Sauerkraut** | Surface film (kahm yeast) | *Pichia*, *Candida*, *Hansenula* | Aerobic film-forming yeasts |
| **Beer** | Sarcina sickness (turbidity, acidity) | *Pediococcus cerevisiae* | Diacetyl, lactic acid overproduction |
| **Beer** | Acetobacter pellicle (vinegar) | *Acetobacter aceti* | Oxidation of ethanol → acetic acid |
| **Beer** | Ropiness (viscous pour) | *Acetobacter* / *Pediococcus* (capsular polysaccharide) | Exopolysaccharide secretion |
| **Wine** | Film yeast (velum, "flor" spoilage) | *Candida vini*, *Pichia*, *Hansenula* | Oxidative film, acetaldehyde |
| **Wine** | Mousiness (urinous taint) | *Brettanomyces* + some LAB | Tetrahydropyridine synthesis |
| **Wine** | Brett taint (phenolic, smoky) | *Brettanomyces bruxellensis* | 4-Ethylphenol, 4-ethylguaiacol |
| **Cider** | Cider sickness / "framboisé" | *Zymomonas mobilis* | Aldehyde + CO₂ from fructose |
| **Bread (sourdough)** | Ropiness (sticky stringy crumb) | *Bacillus subtilis* spore germination | Polyglutamate capsule production |
| **Bread (sourdough)** | Mold spots | *Aspergillus*, *Penicillium*, *Neurospora* ("red bread mold") | Surface mycelial growth |
| **Soy Sauce** | Film yeast | *Zygosaccharomyces rouxii*, *Candida* | Osmotolerant film former |
| **Vinegar** | "Vinegar eels" (turbidity) | *Turbatrix aceti* (nematode) + *Acetobacter* overgrowth | Mother formation |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Spoilage Kinetics

> [!NOTE]
> While food spoilage is biological, **KTU expects you to apply quantitative kinetics** to predict shelf life. The following equations are non-negotiable.

### The Q₁₀ Rule (Temperature Coefficient)

$$
Q_{10} = \frac{\text{Rate at } (T+10)\,^{\circ}\text{C}}{\text{Rate at } T\,^{\circ}\text{C}}
$$

For most microbial spoilage reactions, **Q₁₀ ≈ 2 to 3**.

**Shelf-life relationship:**

$$
\theta_{2} = \theta_{1} \times Q_{10}^{\left(\frac{T_{1}-T_{2}}{10}\right)}
$$

where $\theta$ = shelf life (days), $T$ = storage temperature (°C).

### Arrhenius Equation for Spoilage Rate

$$
k = A \cdot e^{-E_{a}/RT}
$$

where:
- $k$ = rate constant (day⁻¹)
- $A$ = pre-exponential factor (day⁻¹)
- $E_{a}$ = activation energy (J/mol)
- $R$ = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** (universal gas constant)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse $R$ (J/mol·K) with the gas constant in different units. **Always convert to Kelvin first** by adding 273.15 to Celsius.

### Decimal Reduction Time (D-value)

$$
D = \frac{t}{\log_{10} N_{0} - \log_{10} N_{t}}
$$

Used to express inactivation, but adapted in spoilage context for microbial growth lag/load estimation.

### Water Activity Threshold (Critical for Osmotolerant Spoilage)

| **Microorganism Group** | **Minimum a_w for Growth** |
|---|---|
| Most bacteria | 0.91 |
| Most yeasts | 0.88 |
| Most molds | 0.80 |
| *Zygosaccharomyces rouxii* (osmophilic) | 0.62 |
| *Aspergillus* (xerophilic) | 0.65 |

**Engineering Implication:** Soy sauce (a_w ≈ 0.80) is spoiled primarily by *Z. rouxii*, not by typical *Saccharomyces*.

---

## 2.4 Hurdle Technology — The Defense Map

> [!TIP]
> **Claus Hipp's Hurdle Principle:** A fermented food is stable because multiple **hurdles (inhibitory factors)** are stacked. Spoilage occurs when the **sum of hurdle heights falls below the microbial tolerance threshold.**

```
         pH    a_w   Salt   Temp   Starter   O₂   Preservatives
          ↓     ↓     ↓      ↓      ↓        ↓        ↓
   ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │ ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓ │  ←  Microbe
   │ ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓ │   Tolerance
   │ ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░ │  ← Hurdle Stack
   │ ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░ │
   └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
             ▓  Tolerance height   ░  Hurdle height
```

**Spoilage occurs when:** `Σ(hurdle heights) < tolerance of contaminant`.
