# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between foodborne infection and foodborne intoxication. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Foodborne Infection | Foodborne Intoxication |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Live pathogen grows in host | Preformed toxin ingested |
| **Infective Dose** | Requires large number of cells | Toxin dose matters, not cells |
| **Onset** | Hours to days (long) | Minutes to hours (short) |
| **Food Holding** | Pathogen grows during storage | Toxin produced during storage |
| **Antibiotic Use** | Sometimes helpful | Not effective (toxin remains) |
| **Example** | **Salmonellosis** (*Salmonella enterica*) | **Staphylococcal food poisoning** (*S. aureus* enterotoxin) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Marking Tip (3 marks):** Examiner awards 1 mark for definition of infection, 1 mark for definition of intoxication, and 1 mark for the example. Do NOT skip the example — it carries its own mark.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: List any three virulence factors of *Listeria monocytogenes* and explain how it survives refrigeration temperatures.**

**Model Answer:**

**Three Virulence Factors:**
1. **Listeriolysin O (LLO)** — A pore-forming, cholesterol-dependent toxin that lyses the phagosomal membrane, allowing the bacterium to escape into the cytoplasm. (1 mark)
2. **ActA protein** — Mimics host WASP, recruits Arp2/3 complex, nucleates actin polymerization → forms "actin comet tail" for intracellular motility and cell-to-cell spread. (1 mark)
3. **Internalins (InlA, InlB)** — Surface proteins that bind E-cadherin and Met receptors on intestinal epithelial cells, enabling active invasion of non-phagocytic cells. (1 mark)

**Refrigeration Survival:**
*Listeria* is a **psychrotroph** with growth range **0.5°C to 45°C**. It maintains **membrane fluidity** at low temperatures by increasing the proportion of **unsaturated, short-chain fatty acids** in its cytoplasmic membrane. It also produces **cold-shock proteins (CspA-like)** that enable continued translation at low temperatures, and **cold-active enzymes** maintain metabolic activity at refrigeration temperatures. This makes it uniquely dangerous in **refrigerated RTE foods** (deli meats, soft cheeses, smoked fish).

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Module 4 Model Paper]`

### Question A (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: Enumerate the six pathotypes of diarrheagenic *Escherichia coli*. Describe in detail the pathogenesis, clinical features, and food vehicles of Enterohemorrhagic *E. coli* (EHEC) O157:H7.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Six Pathotypes of Diarrheagenic E. coli (3 marks):**

1. **Enterohemorrhagic E. coli (EHEC/STEC)** — produces Shiga toxin
2. **Enterotoxigenic E. coli (ETEC)** — LT and ST toxins; Traveler's diarrhea
3. **Enteropathogenic E. coli (EPEC)** — attaching and effacing lesions; infants
4. **Enteroinvasive E. coli (EIEC)** — Shigella-like invasion
5. **Enteroaggregative E. coli (EAEC)** — aggregative adherence; persistent diarrhea
6. **Diffusely adherent E. coli (DAEC)** — Afa/Dr adhesins

**(ii) Pathogenesis of EHEC O157:H7 (2 marks):**

1. Ingestion via contaminated beef (especially undercooked ground beef), raw milk, sprouts, apple juice, contaminated water.
2. **Acid tolerance** allows gastric passage.
3. **Colonization of colonic mucosa** via the **LEE (Locus of Enterocyte Effacement) pathogenicity island**.
4. The **T3SS (Type III Secretion System)** injects effector proteins (**Tir, EspA, EspB, EspD**) into host enterocytes.
5. **Tir (Translocated Intimin Receptor)** inserts into host membrane, binds **Intimin** on bacterial surface → **intimate attachment**.
6. Host cell cytoskeleton rearranges → **Attaching and Effacing (A/E) lesions** with loss of microvilli (pedestal formation).
7. **Shiga toxin (Stx1, Stx2)** — phage-encoded A-B₅ subunit toxin. B subunits bind **globotriaosylceramide (Gb3)** on endothelial cells, A subunit cleaves 28S rRNA → inhibits protein synthesis → cell death.

**(iii) Clinical Features (1 mark):**

- **Asymptomatic carriage** (low dose)
- **Bloody diarrhea** (hemorrhagic colitis) with severe abdominal cramps
- **Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome (HUS)** — triad: microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, acute renal failure (5–10% of cases, especially in children under 5)
- **Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura (TTP)** in adults

**(iv) Food Vehicles (1 mark):**

- Undercooked **ground beef** (primary)
- **Raw milk** and unpasteurized dairy
- **Sprouts** (alfalfa, radish)
- **Lettuce, spinach**
- **Contaminated water**
- **Apple cider** (unpasteurized)
- **Salami, dry-cured salami** outbreaks reported

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — losing 2 marks:** Students often confuse STEC with ETEC. **STEC** = Shiga toxin + A/E lesions + bloody diarrhea + HUS (no enterotoxin). **ETEC** = heat-labile and heat-stable enterotoxins + watery diarrhea (no HUS). The acronym STEC = **S**higa **T**oxin-producing **E**. **C**oli, NOT enterotoxigenic. Examiners specifically check this distinction.

---

### Question A (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Describe the etiology, pathogenesis, and laboratory diagnosis of Brucellosis in detail. Add a note on its prevention.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Etiology (2 marks):**

- **Genus:** *Brucella* — **Gram-negative, non-motile, non-spore-forming, facultative intracellular coccobacilli**.
- **Key Species:**
  - ***B. melitensis*** (sheep/goats) — **most virulent; most common human pathogen**
  - ***B. abortus*** (cattle)
  - ***B. suis*** (swine)
  - ***B. canis*** (dogs)
  - ***B. neotomae*** (rodents) — rarely infects humans
- All are considered **bioterrorism agents** (Category B).
- Slowly growing; fastidious; require CO₂ for primary isolation of *B. abortus*.

**(ii) Pathogenesis (3 marks):**

1. **Entry** via ingestion (unpasteurized dairy, undercooked meat), inhalation (aerosols from contaminated animal products), or direct contact (abraded skin, mucous membranes).
2. **Phagocytosis** by neutrophils and macrophages; **survives intracellularly** by:
   - Inhibiting phagosome-lysosome fusion
   - Producing **superoxide dismutase, catalase** to resist oxidative burst
   - Producing **guanylate monophosphate (cGMP)** that suppresses macrophage activation
3. **Lymphatic spread** to regional lymph nodes.
4. **Hematogenous dissemination** to reticuloendothelial system (liver, spleen, bone marrow, kidneys).
5. **Granuloma formation** — caseating, similar to tuberculosis; the hallmark histopathological feature.
6. **Hypersensitivity** — Delayed-Type Hypersensitivity (Type IV) contributes to chronicity and granulomatous pathology.
7. **Clinical phases:**
   - **Acute phase:** Undulant fever (sweats, chills, myalgia, fatigue), hepatosplenomegaly.
   - **Chronic phase (>1 year):** Endocarditis, osteomyelitis, arthritis, neurobrucellosis, depression.

**(iii) Laboratory Diagnosis (1 mark):**

- **Specimen:** Blood (acute), bone marrow, lymph node aspirate, serum.
- **Direct Microscopy:** Gram-negative coccobacilli (low sensitivity).
- **Culture:** **Castañeda medium** (biphasic blood agar/broth) or Brucella agar; **slow growth (2–4 weeks)** under 5–10% CO₂; small, smooth, glistening colonies.
- **Identification:** Positive oxidase, catalase, urease; H₂S production (*B. abortus*); differential dye inhibition (Thionine, Basic fuchsin).
- **Serology:**
  - **Serum Agglutination Test (SAT)** — Standard test; titer ≥ 1:160 suggests infection.
  - **2-Mercaptoethanol (2-ME) test** — differentiates IgM (acute) from IgG (chronic).
  - **ELISA, Coombs test** for chronic cases.
- **Molecular:** PCR targeting **BCSP31**, **IS711** gene.
- **Brucellin skin test** (delayed hypersensitivity).

**(iv) Prevention (1 mark):**

- **Pasteurization** of all milk and dairy products (72°C for 15 sec — HTST).
- **Vaccination of animals** (Rev-1, S19, RB51 strains).
- **Culling** of infected herds.
- **Personal protective equipment (PPE)** for abattoir workers, veterinarians, farmers.
- **Quarantine and test-slaughter** policies.
- **Education** regarding unpasteurized dairy consumption.
- **No human vaccine** currently in widespread use.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — losing 1–2 marks:** Students confuse the **undulant fever** of *Brucella* with the **continuous/remittent fever** of *Salmonella* Typhi. *Brucella* = undulant (waves of fever over days to weeks). Also, do NOT confuse *Brucella*'s slow growth (2–4 weeks) with the rapid growth of *E. coli* (overnight). The 2-ME test distinction between IgM (acute) and IgG (chronic) is a KTU favourite.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Module 4 Model Paper]`

### Question B (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: Discuss in detail the various foodborne viral infections. Highlight the structural, epidemiological, and clinical features of Norovirus and Hepatitis A virus.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Foodborne Viral Infections — Overview (2 marks):**

Foodborne viruses cause **non-bacterial gastroenteritis and hepatitis** and are transmitted primarily via the **fecal-oral route**. Common features:
- **Non-enveloped** → highly resistant to environmental stressors, gastric acid, bile salts, detergents.
- **Low infective dose** (10–100 virions).
- **Cannot replicate in food** → food acts as a passive vehicle.
- **Primary concentration** in bivalve mollusks (filter feeders).
- **Top viruses:** Norovirus, Rotavirus, Hepatitis A, Enteric Adenovirus (types 40, 41).

**(ii) Norovirus (2.5 marks):**

| Feature | Description |
|---|---|
| **Family** | *Caliciviridae* |
| **Genome** | Positive-sense, single-stranded RNA (+ssRNA) |
| **Capsid** | Non-enveloped, **icosahedral**, cup-like depressions (calyx = cup) |
| **Size** | 27–32 nm |
| **Genogroups** | GI, GII (GII.4 most common in humans), GIII, GIV, GV |
| **Infective Dose** | **10–100 viral particles** |
| **Incubation** | **12–48 hours** |
| **Symptoms** | Acute **vomiting** (often projectile) + watery diarrhea; lasts 24–72 h; self-limiting |
| **Vehicle** | Shellfish (oysters), salad, fresh fruit, contaminated water, ice, food handler contamination |
| **Reservoir** | Humans (only) |
| **Stability** | Resistant to freezing, mild heat (60°C/30 min), chlorination, alcohol-based sanitizers |
| **Outbreaks** | Cruise ships, hospitals, schools, catered events, restaurants |

**(iii) Hepatitis A Virus (2.5 marks):**

| Feature | Description |
|---|---|
| **Family** | *Picornaviridae* (Hepatovirus genus) |
| **Genome** | Positive-sense, single-stranded RNA (+ssRNA) |
| **Capsid** | Non-enveloped, icosahedral, 27–32 nm |
| **Stability** | **Highly stable** to acid, detergents, drying, low temperatures |
| **Inactivation** | Heat 85°C for 1 min; UV, chlorine (1 ppm free chlorine for 30 min) |
| **Incubation** | **15–50 days (average 28 days)** — long incubation = lengthy outbreak tracing |
| **Symptoms** | Pre-icteric phase (fever, malaise, anorexia, nausea); Icteric phase (jaundice, dark urine, hepatomegaly); usually self-limiting; **no chronicity** (unlike HBV/HCV) |
| **Vehicle** | Shellfish (oysters, clams, mussels), raw produce, contaminated water, food handlers |
| **Reservoir** | Humans only; **anthroponotic** |
| **Marker** | Anti-HAV IgM (acute), IgG (immunity); HAV RNA in stool |
| **Prevention** | Improved sanitation, hand-washing, **Hepatitis A vaccine** (inactivated), immune globulin (post-exposure) |

---

### Question B (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Compare and contrast the etiology, pathogenesis, clinical manifestations, and food vehicles of Salmonellosis and Campylobacteriosis.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Comparative Table (5 marks):**

| Feature | **Salmonellosis** | **Campylobacteriosis** |
|---|---|---|
| **Etiology** | *Salmonella enterica* (subspecies *enterica*) | *Campylobacter jejuni*, *C. coli* |
| **Gram Stain** | Gram-negative | Gram-negative |
| **Morphology** | Motile, peritrichous flagella, rod | Curved/S-shaped/spiral rod, **single polar flagellum** |
| **Oxygen Requirement** | Facultative anaerobe | **Microaerophilic (5% O₂)** |
| **Optimal Growth T°** | 35–37°C | **42°C (thermophilic)** |
| **Infective Dose** | $10^5$ to $10^8$ CFU (high) | **~500 CFU (low)** |
| **Reservoir** | Poultry, swine, cattle, pets (reptiles) | Poultry (primary), cattle, sheep, wild birds |
| **Vehicle** | Eggs, poultry meat, dairy, fresh produce | Undercooked poultry, raw milk, contaminated water |
| **Pathogenesis** | Acid tolerance → SPI-1/SPI-2 T3SS → invade M cells → multiply in macrophages → enterocolitis or bacteremia | Darting motility + chemotaxis → invade mucus → adhesion (PEB1, CadF) → CDT toxin → bloody diarrhea |
| **Invasion** | Facultative intracellular (within macrophages) | Facultative intracellular (within enterocytes) |
| **Incubation** | **6–72 hours (gastroenteritis)**; 7–14 d (typhoid) | **2–5 days** |
| **Symptoms** | Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea (often non-bloody), fever, cramps | Bloody diarrhea, severe abdominal cramps, fever |
| **Sequelae** | Reactive arthritis; chronic carrier state (*S.* Typhi) | **Guillain-Barré Syndrome (GBS)**; reactive arthritis (Reiter's) |
| **Mortality** | Low (high in immunocompromised and typhoid) | Low overall |

**(ii) Key Contrasts (2 marks):**

1. **Infective Dose:** *Salmonella* requires orders of magnitude higher dose ($10^5$–$10^8$ CFU) than *Campylobacter* (~500 CFU). This makes cross-contamination with raw poultry particularly dangerous for *Campylobacter* even at low levels.

2. **Oxygen Requirement:** *Salmonella* grows easily under ambient conditions; *Campylobacter* requires **microaerophilic conditions (5% O₂, 10% CO₂)** — this is why *Campylobacter* is technically difficult to culture (special selective media like Skirrow's, Campy agar, Preston agar).

3. **Temperature Optimum:** *Salmonella* grows at 35–37°C; *Campylobacter* prefers **42°C** (chicken body temperature) — this is why poultry is the natural reservoir.

4. **Post-infectious Complications:** *Salmonella* → reactive arthritis and chronic *S.* Typhi carriage; *Campylobacter* → **Guillain-Barré Syndrome (GBS)** is the most feared sequela. GBS is an autoimmune demyelinating polyneuropathy that can cause paralysis; *C. jejuni* is the **leading antecedent infection** of GBS worldwide.

5. **Selective Advantages:** *Salmonella*'s SPI-1 and SPI-2 Type III Secretion Systems allow survival within macrophages, enabling systemic disease (typhoid). *Campylobacter* lacks these sophisticated systems and remains primarily intestinal.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — losing 2 marks:** Do not confuse Campylobacter's microaerophilic requirement with anaerobic growth. Microaerophilic ≠ anaerobic. Also, students often wrongly state that *Campylobacter* does not invade cells — in fact, it is **facultative intracellular** within enterocytes, but its invasion is less sophisticated than *Salmonella*'s. The 42°C optimum vs. 37°C is a KTU favourite one-mark question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Foodborne Infections)

- [ ] **Salmonellosis:** *S. enterica*; high dose ($10^5$–$10^8$); SPI-1/SPI-2 T3SS; eggs/poultry; *S.* Typhi → enteric fever; rose spots; chronic gallbladder carriage.
- [ ] **Listeriosis:** *L. monocytogenes*; **psychrotroph (0.5–45°C)**; ActA + LLO + InlA/InlB; RTE foods; 20–30% mortality; teratogenic.
- [ ] **Pathogenic E. coli:** Six pathotypes — EHEC, ETEC, EPEC, EIEC, EAEC, DAEC. **O157:H7** = STEC = Shiga toxin + A/E lesions + HUS; low dose (10–100 CFU).
- [ ] **Shigellosis:** *Shigella* spp.; **humans only**; IcsA actin motility; **lowest infective dose (10–200 CFU)**; *S. dysenteriae* produces Shiga toxin.
- [ ] **Campylobacteriosis:** *C. jejuni*; **microaerophilic, 42°C, darting motility, single polar flagellum**; poultry; **GBS** sequela.
- [ ] **Enteric Viruses:** **Non-enveloped, low dose, environmentally stable.** Norovirus (24–48 h onset, vomiting predominant, cruise ships); HAV (15–50 day onset, jaundice, shellfish, vaccine available).
- [ ] **Brucellosis:** *B. melitensis* most virulent; **undulant fever**; **granulomas**; unpasteurized dairy; abattoir worker risk; Castañeda medium; SAT, 2-ME test.
- [ ] **Q Fever:** *Coxiella burnetii*; **obligate intracellular**; **1–10 organisms** (most infectious); inhalation; **aerosolized from birth fluids**; Phase I/II variation; chronic endocarditis.
- [ ] **Cross-cutting Themes:** **Psychrotrophs** (Listeria); **Low dose killers** (STEC, Shigella, Norovirus, Coxiella); **Obligate intracellular** (Coxiella); **Facultative intracellular** (Listeria, Salmonella, Brucella, Campylobacter).
- [ ] **Mandatory Drug Class Awareness:** Antibiotics are **CONTRAINDICATED** in EHEC O157:H7 (increase Shiga toxin release → HUS risk). Used in invasive listeriosis, brucellosis (Doxy + Rifampin/Streptomycin).
- [ ] **HACPP-Relevant:** *Listeria* → 4°C cold chain; STEC → 70°C+ cooking; *Coxiella* → pasteurization 72°C/15 sec; HAV/Norovirus → hand-washing, shellfish sourcing.
- [ ] **Numerical Mnemonics to Memorize:**
  - *STEC* O157:H7 dose: $10$–$10^2$ CFU
  - *Shigella* dose: $10$–$2 \times 10^2$ CFU
  - *Campylobacter* dose: $\sim 5 \times 10^2$ CFU
  - *Salmonella* dose: $10^5$–$10^8$ CFU
  - *Coxiella* dose: $1$–$10$ CFU
  - *Norovirus* dose: $10$–$10^2$ virions
  - *Listeria* mortality: $20$–$30$% (highest of foodborne pathogens)

### Key One-Liners for Last-Minute Revision

1. "**T**hree p**s**y**c**hrotrophs of M**o**dule 4: **L**isteria, **Y**ersinia, and **B**acillus (non-module) — but *Listeria* is the prime one."
2. "***E. coli* O157:H7** is the **odd *E. coli*** — it's the only major one that produces a **systemic toxin** (Shiga) and is **sorbitol-negative** on SMAC agar."
3. "**Shigella** is *Listeria*'s intracellular cousin: both polymerize actin, but Shigella uses **IcsA** while Listeria uses **ActA**."
4. "**Norovirus** = winter vomiting disease, **cruise ship virus**; **Rotavirus** = infant diarrhea, both are **non-enveloped + resistant**."
5. "**Brucellosis** = undulant fever; **Q Fever** = atypical pneumonia from aerosols — they are often tested together because both are **zoonotic intracellular bacteria**."
6. "**Coxiella burnetii** has the **lowest known infective dose** (1–10 organisms) of any pathogen — make a note of this for the 'one-mark' surprise question."
7. "**HAV** is the **only Module 4 disease with a widely available vaccine** (inactivated); **HBV/HCV** are NOT foodborne (parenteral transmission)."
8. "**RTE foods + refrigeration = Listeria risk**; **raw ground beef + child = HUS risk**; **shellfish + jaundice = HAV**; **dairy + undulant fever = Brucella**."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy Tip:** In the 14-mark question, the examiner is testing your ability to (a) enumerate facts, (b) draw clear connections, and (c) demonstrate applied knowledge of food safety. Always structure your answer as: **Etiology → Pathogenesis → Clinical Features → Lab Diagnosis → Prevention/Control**. A well-structured answer with a comparative table and a pathogenesis flowchart description typically scores **12–14 marks** even with limited writing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Foodborne Intoxications - Staphylococcal, Botulism, Mycotoxicosis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT303 - FOOD MICROBIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Foodborne Intoxications - Staphylococcal, Botulism, Mycotoxicosis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🍽️ Foodborne Intoxications — Staphylococcal, Botulism & Mycotoxicosis

## 1.1 Defining the Core Concept (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FOODBORNE INTOXICATION (Formal KTU Definition):**
> A foodborne disease caused by the **ingestion of preformed toxic metabolites (toxins)** synthesized by microorganisms that have previously grown in the food item. The living causative organism **does NOT need to be present** in the host at the time of illness — only its toxin is required to trigger pathogenesis. This is fundamentally distinct from a foodborne **infection**, where the live pathogen itself colonizes the gastrointestinal tract.

Three classical intoxications are mandated by the KTU 2024 syllabus under this module:

| Intoxication | Causative Agent | Nature of Toxin | Classical Example |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **Staphylococcal** | *Staphylococcus aureus* | Heat-stable **enterotoxin** | Cream pastries, ham |
| **Botulism** | *Clostridium botulinum* | Heat-labile **neurotoxin** | Home-canned vegetables |
| **Mycotoxicosis** | *Aspergillus*, *Fusarium*, *Penicillium* spp. | Heat-stable **mycotoxin** | Moldy grains, peanuts |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Poisoned Leftover" 🍲

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of intoxication like this:** Imagine a chef (the microbe) cooks in a restaurant kitchen (the food). Before you even arrive, the chef has already *secreted a poisonous seasoning* into every dish. When you eat the food, you get sick — but **you never meet the chef**. The poison is the only thing that reaches you.
>
> - **Staphylococcal = a quick-acting "vomit-inducing seasoning"** (works within hours)
> - **Botulism = a slow-acting "nerve-paralyzing venom"** (works within a day, but devastating)
> - **Mycotoxicosis = a chronic "silent poison"** released by invisible molds on stored grains (works over months/years, may cause cancer)

This single analogy unifies the entire module — every intoxication below is essentially a story of *who* the chef is, *what* poison they leave behind, and *how fast* it strikes.

---

## 1.3 Why KTU Emphasizes This Module

> [!IMPORTANT]
> According to KTU 2024 Course Outcome mapping for **PCFTT303**, this topic directly contributes to:
> - **CO2:** *Identify the role of microorganisms in food spoilage and food-borne diseases.*
> - **CO3:** *Apply microbiological principles to ensure food safety.*
>
> Expect **direct 3-mark definitional questions** and **14-mark descriptive/illustrative questions** on this content in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

---

## 1.4 Quick Snapshot — The Three Intoxications at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic — "SH-B-M" (Short-Hands, Big-Mechanism):**
> - **S**taphylococcal → **S**peed (rapid onset, superantigen-like enterotoxin)
> - **B**otulism → **B**locker (blocks acetylcholine → flaccid paralysis)
> - **M**ycotoxicosis → **M**alignancy (chronic exposure → cancer, hepatotoxicity)

The **incubation period**, **toxin dose**, and **target organ** form the trinity of differentiation the examiner will test.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis — Mechanisms, Toxins & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Staphylococcal Food Poisoning (SFP)

### 2.1.1 The Organism — *Staphylococcus aureus*

- **Morphology:** Gram-positive, non-motile, non-sporulating cocci arranged in **irregular grape-like clusters**.
- **Physiology:** Facultative anaerobe; grows best at **30–37 °C**; tolerates **7.5–10 % NaCl** (halotolerant).
- **Habitat:** Anterior nares (40–50 % healthy carriers), skin, hair, infected cuts. Hence, the classic source is the **food handler**.

### 2.1.2 The Enterotoxin

> [!IMPORTANT]
> *S. aureus* produces **Staphylococcal Enterotoxins (SEs)** — a family of at least **seven serologically distinct superantigens** designated **SEA, SEB, SEC, SED, SEE, SEG, SEH**. SEA is the most common cause of food poisoning outbreaks worldwide.

**Key properties of the enterotoxin:**
1. **Heat-stable:** Resists boiling at 100 °C for several minutes; not inactivated by ordinary cooking.
2. **Protease-resistant:** Survives stomach acid and intestinal proteases.
3. **Superantigenic:** Binds MHC class II and TCR nonspecifically → massive cytokine release.
4. **Emetic dose:** As little as **< 1 µg** of toxin can induce symptoms in humans.

### 2.1.3 Pathogenesis (How it Makes You Sick)

```
Enterotoxin ingested → Survives gastric digestion → Binds emetic receptors
on abdominal visceral afferent nerves → Stimulates vomiting centre (medulla)
→ Nausea + Vomiting + Retching within 1–6 hours
```

### 2.1.4 Clinical Picture

| Feature | Detail |
|---------|--------|
| **Onset** | 1 – 6 hours after ingestion |
| **Duration** | 24 – 48 hours (self-limiting) |
| **Symptoms** | Nausea, projectile vomiting, abdominal cramps, **diarrhoea** (in ~50 % cases) |
| **Mortality** | Almost zero in healthy adults; fatal in elderly/immunocompromised |
| **Infective dose of toxin** | < 1 µg (one of the lowest in food microbiology) |

### 2.1.5 Implicated Foods

> [!NOTE]
> **The "HAM & EGGS" mnemonic** for high-risk SFP foods:
> - **H**am and cured meats
> - **A**mayonnaise-based salads
> - **M**ilk and dairy (esp. soft cheeses)
> - **E**ggs (custards, hollandaise)
> - **G**elatin desserts
> - **G**ravy and cream-filled pastries
> - **S**alads (potato, macaroni, tuna)

---

## 2.2 Botulism

### 2.2.1 The Organism — *Clostridium botulinum*

- **Morphology:** Gram-positive, **motile**, **spore-forming**, encapsulated rods.
- **Physiology:** **Obligate anaerobe** — its very name derives from Latin *botulus* (sausage), recalling the 19th-century sausage-poisoning outbreaks.
- **Spores:** Among the most heat-resistant in the bacterial world; require **121 °C / 15 min at 15 psi** in a pressure retort for destruction — ordinary boiling will NOT kill them.

### 2.2.2 The Neurotoxin — "The Most Potent Biological Poison Known"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Botulinum toxin is the **most lethal naturally occurring substance** known to science. The **lethal dose 50 (LD₅₀)** for an adult human is estimated at **~1–2 µg by oral route** and **~1–2 ng by intravenous route**. A single gram of crystalline toxin, if dispersed optimally, could theoretically kill **over one million people**.

**The seven toxin types:** A, B, C, D, E, F, G — distinguished antigenically.
- **Human botulism:** Caused by types **A, B, E** (and rarely F).
- **Type A** → most severe, predominantly western United States.
- **Type E** → associated with fish and marine mammals.
- **Type B** → predominantly Europe (especially from home-preserved meats).

### 2.2.3 Mechanism of Action (KTU High-Yield)

```
Toxin ingested → Absorbed from intestine → Binds irreversibly to
presynaptic cholinergic nerve terminals → Cleaves SNARE proteins
(synaptobrevin, SNAP-25, syntaxin) → Blocks fusion of
acetylcholine-containing vesicles → NO acetylcholine released
into neuromuscular junction → FLACCID PARALYSIS
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter for diagnosis?** Botulism is a **descending symmetrical paralysis** — it begins in the cranial nerves (diplopia, dysphagia, dysarthria) and descends to the limbs and diaphragm. Respiratory failure is the usual cause of death.

### 2.2.4 Types of Botulism (KTU Examiner Favourite)

| Type | Population | Source |
|------|------------|--------|
| **Foodborne (classical)** | Adults | Home-canned low-acid foods (corn, beans, beets, asparagus) |
| **Infant botulism** | < 1 year | **Honey** (spores germinate in immature gut) |
| **Wound botulism** | Trauma / IV drug users | Spores germinate in anaerobic wound tissue |
| **Inhalation botulism** | (Bioterrorism) | Aerosolized toxin |
| **Iatrogenic** | Cosmetic misuse | Overdose of Botox injections |

### 2.2.5 Clinical Picture

| Feature | Detail |
|---------|--------|
| **Onset** | 12 – 48 hours (range 6 h – 8 days) |
| **First signs** | Diplopia (double vision), blurred vision, ptosis |
| **Progression** | Dysphagia → dysarthria → descending limb weakness → respiratory paralysis |
| **GI symptoms** | Often ABSENT (unlike SFP) |
| **Sensory function** | Intact (no numbness) |
| **Fever** | Typically absent |
| **Mortality** | 5 – 10 % with modern supportive care; **60–70 % untreated** |

### 2.2.6 Treatment

1. **Trivalent antitoxin (A, B, E)** — administered ASAP; neutralizes circulating toxin only.
2. **Supportive ventilation** — mechanical respiration is often life-saving.
3. **Gastric lavage / enema** — to remove unabsorbed toxin.
4. **Guanidine hydrochloride** — older adjunct that enhances acetylcholine release.

---

## 2.3 Mycotoxicosis

### 2.3.1 Defining Mycotoxins

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mycotoxins** are low-molecular-weight (**MW 200–800 Da**) secondary metabolites of filamentous fungi (molds) that are toxic to vertebrates in low concentrations. They are **chemically stable**, **heat-stable**, and contaminate foods primarily **pre-harvest** (in the field) and during **improper storage**.

The five major mycotoxin groups mandated by the KTU syllabus are summarised below.

### 2.3.2 The Major Mycotoxins — At a Glance

| Mycotoxin | Producing Fungi | Major Substrate | Primary Toxic Effect |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|----------------------|
| **Aflatoxin B₁** | *Aspergillus flavus*, *A. parasiticus* | Peanuts, maize, tree nuts, cottonseed | **Hepatocellular carcinoma** (Group 1 IARC carcinogen) |
| **Aflatoxin M₁** | (Metabolite in lactating mammals) | Milk and dairy | Hepatotoxic, carcinogenic |
| **Ochratoxin A** | *Aspergillus ochraceus*, *Penicillium verrucosum* | Cereals, coffee, wine, dried fruits | **Nephrotoxic**; possible human carcinogen (Group 2B) |
| **Fumonisin B₁** | *Fusarium verticillioides* (= *moniliforme*) | Maize (corn) | **Equine leukoencephalomalacia**; human oesophageal cancer |
| **Trichothecenes** (e.g., DON = Vomitoxin; T-2 toxin) | *Fusarium graminearum*, *F. sporotrichioides* | Wheat, barley, maize | **Dermal necrosis**, haemorrhage, immunosuppression, vomiting |
| **Zearalenone** | *Fusarium graminearum* | Maize, wheat | Oestrogenic effects → reproductive disorders in swine |
| **Patulin** | *Penicillium expansum*, *Aspergillus* spp. | Rotting apples, apple juice | GI haemorrhage, oedema; **possible carcinogen** |

### 2.3.3 Aflatoxin — The Archetype (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aflatoxin B₁ (AFB₁)** is the most potent naturally occurring hepatocarcinogen ever identified. It was the causative agent in the **1974 India "hepatitis-X" outbreak** in Gujarat and Maharashtra, where consumption of heavily contaminated maize claimed **over 100 lives** and caused acute hepatic failure.

**Structure:** A difuranocoumarin (bis-furan ring fused to a coumarin moiety).

**Mechanism of Carcinogenesis:**
```
AFB₁ ingested → Cytochrome P450 activation in liver → AFB₁-8,9-epoxide
(ultra-reactive) → Covalent binding to N7-guanine of DNA
→ AFB₁-N7-Gua adduct → Apurinic site → G→T transversion
at codon 249 of p53 tumour-suppressor gene
→ UNCONTROLLED HEPATOCYTE PROLIFERATION → HCC
```

### 2.3.4 Regulatory Limits (Important for KTU Numerical Questions)

| Commodity | Maximum Aflatoxin Limit (as per Codex/FSSAI) |
|-----------|-----------------------------------------------|
| **Total aflatoxins in food** | 20 µg/kg (ppb) |
| **Aflatoxin B₁ in food** | 10 µg/kg (ppb) |
| **Aflatoxin M₁ in milk** | 0.5 µg/kg (ppb) |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋

> [!NOTE]
> Although this module is qualitative, certain **dose-response values** are high-yield for KTU numerical/conceptual problems.

### 2.4.1 Toxic Dose Constants

| Parameter | Value | Significance |
|-----------|-------|--------------|
| **Staphylococcal enterotoxin emetic dose** | < 1 µg | Lowest toxin dose in food microbiology |
| **Botulinum toxin oral LD₅₀ (human)** | ≈ 1 – 2 µg | Most lethal natural toxin |
| **Botulinum toxin IV LD₅₀ (human)** | ≈ 1 – 2 ng | Most lethal by parenteral route |
| **Aflatoxin B₁ LD₅₀ (rat, oral)** | ≈ 1 – 18 mg/kg body weight | Acute hepatotoxic dose |
| **Fumonisin B₁ no-observed-effect level (NOEL, rat)** | 0.2 mg/kg b.w. per day | Used for TDI calculation |

### 2.4.2 Calculating Toxin Dose in a Food Sample

The general formula used in food safety assessment:

$$D_{toxin} = \frac{C_{toxin} \times M_{food}}{M_{body}}$$

where $D_{toxin}$ is the toxin dose per kg body weight (mg/kg), $C_{toxin}$ is the toxin concentration in food (mg/kg), $M_{food}$ is the mass of food consumed (kg), and $M_{body}$ is the consumer's body weight (kg).

### 2.4.3 Regulatory Threshold Comparison

$$\text{Compliance} = \begin{cases} \text{ACCEPT} & \text{if } C_{toxin} \leq L_{regulatory} \\ \text{REJECT} & \text{if } C_{toxin} > L_{regulatory} \end{cases}$$

where $L_{regulatory}$ is the Codex/FSSAI maximum permissible limit.

### 2.4.4 Dilution / Carry-Over Factor (Milk from AFB₁ Feed)

$$C_{M_1} = f_{carry} \times C_{B_1}^{feed}$$

where $f_{carry}$ is the **carry-over factor** (typically ≈ 1–3 % of ingested AFB₁ appears as AFM₁ in milk), $C_{M_1}$ is the AFM₁ concentration in milk, and $C_{B_1}^{feed}$ is the AFB₁ concentration in cattle feed.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering / Industry Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Understanding these intoxications is **not academic alone** — it directly governs:
> - **HACCP plan design** in food processing industries (Critical Control Points for *C. botulinum* spore destruction, *Aspergillus* moisture control).
> - **Thermal process calculation** for canned foods (the 12D concept for *C. botulinum* — destruction at 121.1 °C with $F_0 \geq 3$ min).
> - **Mycotoxin mitigation engineering:** Moisture-controlled silos, modified-atmosphere packaging (MAP), UV detoxification, ammoniation of contaminated feed.
> - **Cold-chain validation** in dairy and meat industries to keep *S. aureus* enterotoxin below the emetic threshold.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📐 Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Comparative Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Estimating the Botulinum Toxin Dose in a Home-Canned Product

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation uses the standard toxicological formula given in §2.4.2 to determine whether a contaminated portion would exceed the human oral LD₅₀.

**Problem Statement:**
A 250 g serving of home-canned beans contains *Clostridium botulinum* type A toxin at a concentration of **5 µg/kg**. The consumer is a 70 kg adult. Calculate the toxin dose per kg body weight and compare with the oral LD₅₀ (≈ 1.5 µg per person).

**Step 1 — Identify the variables.**
$C_{toxin} = 5 \; \mu g/kg$, $M_{food} = 250 \; g = 0.25 \; kg$, $M_{body} = 70 \; kg$.

**Step 2 — Compute total toxin mass in the portion.**

$$M_{toxin}^{total} = C_{toxin} \times M_{food} = 5 \;\frac{\mu g}{kg} \times 0.25 \; kg = 1.25 \; \mu g$$

This explains the multiplication: each kg of beans carries 5 µg of toxin, and the 250 g portion is one-quarter of a kilogram.

**Step 3 — Compute the toxin dose per kg body weight.**

$$D_{toxin} = \frac{M_{toxin}^{total}}{M_{body}} = \frac{1.25 \; \mu g}{70 \; kg} \approx 0.0179 \; \mu g/kg \; b.w.$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the lethal dose.**

$$LD_{50}^{human} \approx \frac{1.5 \; \mu g}{70 \; kg} \approx 0.0214 \; \mu g/kg \; b.w.$$

**Step 5 — Compute the safety margin.**

$$\text{Safety Margin} = \frac{LD_{50}}{D_{toxin}} = \frac{0.0214}{0.0179} \approx 1.2$$

**Interpretation:** The portion delivers a dose only ~1.2× below the human oral LD₅₀. The food is **dangerously close to lethal** — a slightly higher contamination level (e.g., 6 µg/kg) or a larger portion (e.g., 500 g) would *exceed* the lethal dose. This shows why even a single spoonful of improperly canned food can be fatal.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Aflatoxin Carry-Over from Cattle Feed to Milk

**Problem Statement:**
Dairy cattle consume feed containing **AFB₁ at 100 µg/kg** at a rate of **10 kg/day**. With a typical **carry-over factor $f_{carry} = 2 \;\%$**, milk yield is **20 L/day**. Calculate the **AFM₁ concentration per litre of milk** and verify whether it complies with the FSSAI limit of **0.5 µg/L**.

**Step 1 — Daily AFB₁ intake.**

$$I_{daily} = C_{feed} \times M_{feed} = 100 \;\frac{\mu g}{kg} \times 10 \; kg/day = 1000 \;\mu g/day$$

**Step 2 — AFM₁ appearing in milk (mass per day).**

$$M_{M_1}^{daily} = f_{carry} \times I_{daily} = 0.02 \times 1000 \;\mu g/day = 20 \;\mu g/day$$

**Step 3 — Concentration per litre of milk.**

$$C_{M_1} = \frac{M_{M_1}^{daily}}{V_{milk}} = \frac{20 \;\mu g/day}{20 \; L/day} = 1.0 \;\frac{\mu g}{L}$$

**Step 4 — Compliance check.**

$$C_{M_1} = 1.0 \; \mu g/L \; > \; L_{FSSAI} = 0.5 \; \mu g/L \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{NON-COMPLIANT}$$

**Step 5 — Maximum permissible feed AFB₁.**

Rearranging the equation to find the feed level that keeps milk within the limit:

$$C_{feed}^{max} = \frac{L_{FSSAI} \times V_{milk}}{f_{carry} \times M_{feed}} = \frac{0.5 \times 20}{0.02 \times 10} = 50 \;\mu g/kg$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Feed AFB₁ must be **≤ 50 µg/kg** (5× stricter than the food limit) to keep milk safe — illustrating why dairy industries maintain stringent **upstream controls** rather than testing only the final milk.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Thermal Death Time (TDT) for *C. botulinum* Spores

**Problem Statement:**
Using the concept of **12D reduction** (i.e., a 12-log-cycle reduction in spore count), calculate the required heating time at **121.1 °C** for a product containing an initial spore load of $N_0 = 10^{6} \; \text{spores}/g$, given that the D-value at 121.1 °C is $D_{121} = 0.21 \; \text{min}$.

**Step 1 — Apply the 12D criterion.**

$$t_{required} = 12 \times D_{121} = 12 \times 0.21 \; min = 2.52 \; min$$

**Step 2 — Verify final spore count.**

$$N_f = N_0 \times 10^{-12} = 10^{6} \times 10^{-12} = 10^{-6} \; \text{spores}/g$$

A fraction of a spore per gram is statistically zero — the product is commercially sterile.

**Step 3 — Convert to the standard $F_0$ value.**

$$F_0 = 2.52 \; min \;\; \text{(at reference temperature 121.1 °C, z = 10 °C)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU "botulinum cook" requirement of $F_0 \geq 3 \; min$ is exactly the **12D concept** expressed in different units — a 12-log reduction of the most heat-resistant *C. botulinum* spores.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Master Comparison Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important table for the 14-mark KTU question**. Memorise the column headings; the examiner will compare any pair of these intoxications.

| Feature | Staphylococcal | Botulism | Mycotoxicosis (Aflatoxin) |
|---------|----------------|----------|---------------------------|
| **Causative agent** | *Staphylococcus aureus* | *Clostridium botulinum* | *Aspergillus flavus / parasiticus* |
| **Type of organism** | Gram (+) coccus, non-sporing | Gram (+) rod, spore-forming | Filamentous fungus (mold) |
| **Oxygen requirement** | Facultative anaerobe | **Obligate anaerobe** | Strict aerobe |
| **Toxin type** | Enterotoxin (SEA, SEB, …) | Neurotoxin (A, B, E, …) | Mycotoxin (AFB₁, etc.) |
| **Toxin heat stability** | **Heat-stable** (survives boiling) | **Heat-labile** (destroyed at 80 °C / 10 min) | **Heat-stable** (survives most cooking) |
| **Site of action** | Vagus nerve / vomiting centre | Presynaptic NMJ (blocks ACh) | Hepatocyte DNA (adduct formation) |
| **Incubation period** | 1 – 6 hours (rapid) | 12 – 48 hours (delayed) | Days to years (chronic) |
| **Symptoms** | Vomiting, nausea, cramps | Flaccid paralysis, diplopia | Hepatotoxicity, carcinoma |
| **Mortality (untreated)** | < 1 % | 60 – 70 % | Variable; long-term cancer risk |
| **Highest-risk foods** | Custards, ham, mayonnaise | Home-canned vegetables, honey (infants) | Peanuts, maize, tree nuts |
| **Key prevention** | Refrigeration, hygiene | Pressure canning, boiling before serving | Moisture control, HACCP |
| **Treatment** | Supportive, fluid replacement | Trivalent antitoxin + ventilation | No specific antidote (avoidance) |
| **Diagnostic clue** | Gram (+) cocci in clusters + many neutrophils | Anaerobic spore-former + toxin assay (mouse bioassay) | HPLC, ELISA for AFB₁ |

---

## 3.5 Decision Logic — How an Examiner Distinguishes the Three

> [!NOTE]
> When a KTU question gives a clinical scenario, use this decision tree:

1. **Onset < 6 hours + vomiting dominant?** → **Staphylococcal**
2. **Onset 12–48 hours + descending paralysis + no fever?** → **Botulism**
3. **Chronic exposure + liver disease + carcinogenicity + mouldy food?** → **Mycotoxicosis**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Pathogenesis Flow of Foodborne Intoxications

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Contaminated Food Ingested] --> B1{What kind of toxin is present?}
    B1 -- Enterotoxin --> C1[Staphylococcal aureus]
    B1 -- Neurotoxin --> C2[Clostridium botulinum]
    B1 -- Mycotoxin --> C3[Aspergillus or Fusarium]

    C1 --> D1[Vagus nerve stimulation]
    D1 --> E1[Vomiting centre in medulla]
    E1 --> F1[Projectile vomiting within 1 to 6 hours]

    C2 --> D2[Absorption in small intestine]
    D2 --> E2[Blocks Acetylcholine release at NMJ]
    E2 --> F2[Flaccid descending paralysis after 12 to 48 hours]

    C3 --> D3[Hepatocyte uptake]
    D3 --> E3[Cytochrome P450 bioactivation]
    E3 --> F3[DNA adduct formation and p53 mutation]
    F3 --> G3[Chronic hepatotoxicity and carcinoma]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Botulinum Toxin Mechanism at the Neuromuscular Junction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Botulinum Toxin Ingested] --> nodeB[Absorbed from Intestine]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Binds Presynaptic Cholinergic Nerve Terminal]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Endocytosis into Neuron]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Cleaves SNARE Proteins: SNAP25, Syntaxin, Synaptobrevin]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Vesicle Fusion Blocked]
    nodeF --> nodeG[No Acetylcholine Release]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Flaccid Paralysis of Skeletal Muscle]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Respiratory Failure if Diaphragm Affected]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Aflatoxin B₁ Carcinogenesis Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    moldA[Mouldy Maize Ingested] --> intB[AFB1 enters Liver via Portal Vein]
    intB --> actC[Cytochrome P450 3A4 Activates AFB1]
    actC --> epoxD[Forms AFB1 8 9 Epoxide]
    epoxD --> bindE[Covalent Binding to N7 Guanine of p53 DNA]
    bindE --> mutF[G to T Transversion at Codon 249]
    mutF --> cancG[Loss of p53 Function]
    cancG --> tumorH[Uncontrolled Hepatocyte Proliferation]
    tumorH --> outI[Hepatocellular Carcinoma]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Classification of Botulism Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootBot[Botulism Classification] --> typeA[Foodborne Classical]
    rootBot --> typeB[Infant Botulism]
    rootBot --> typeC[Wound Botulism]
    rootBot --> typeD[Iatrogenic]
    rootBot --> typeE[Inhalation Bioterrorism]

    typeA -- source --> srcA[Home canned low acid foods]
    typeB -- source --> srcB[Honey spores in infants gut]
    typeC -- source --> srcC[Anaerobic wound tissue]
    typeD -- source --> srcD[Botox injection overdose]
    typeE -- source --> srcE[Aerosolised purified toxin]
```

---

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — HACCP for the Three Intoxications

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CCPCriticalControlPoints
        ccp1[CCP1 Refrigeration below 4 degree celsius for S aureus]
        ccp2[CCP2 Pressure retort 121 degree celsius for C botulinum]
        ccp3[CCP3 Moisture below 13 percent for Aspergillus]
    end

    subgraph LIMRegulatoryLimits
        lim1[SEA less than 1 microgram per portion]
        lim2[Botulinum cook F0 greater than 3 min]
        lim3[AFB1 less than 10 microgram per kg]
    end

    subgraph MONMonitoring
        m1[M1 Swab test for food handlers]
        m2[M2 Mouse bioassay or ELISA for toxin]
        m3[M3 HPLC for aflatoxin]
    end

    subgraph ACTCorrectiveAction
        a1[A1 Discard contaminated lot]
        a2[A2 Administer antitoxin]
        a3[A3 Reject shipment]
    end

    ccp1 --> m1 --> a1
    ccp2 --> m2 --> a2
    ccp3 --> m3 --> a3
    ccp1 --> lim1
    ccp2 --> lim2
    ccp3 --> lim3
```
