# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Define the term "D-value" in food microbiology. A bacterial suspension has an initial count of 10⁴ CFU/mL. After 8 minutes of heating at 70 °C, the count drops to 10¹ CFU/mL. Calculate the D-value at 70 °C.**

**Model Answer:**
> **Definition:** The **D-value (Decimal Reduction Time)** is the time, in minutes, required at a given temperature to reduce the microbial population by **90 %** (i.e., by one log cycle or one decade).

**Calculation:**

$$D = \dfrac{t}{\log_{10} N_0 - \log_{10} N}$$

Substituting:

$$D = \dfrac{8}{\log_{10} 10^{4} - \log_{10} 10^{1}} = \dfrac{8}{4 - 1} = \dfrac{8}{3}$$

$$\boxed{D_{70} \approx 2.67 \text{ min}}$$

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Differentiate between "bacteriostatic" and "bactericidal" actions. Give one example of each in food preservation.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Bacteriostatic | Bactericidal |
|---------|----------------|---------------|
| Action | Inhibits growth & multiplication | Kills the microbial cells |
| Reversibility | Effect is reversible (cells resume growth when agent removed) | Irreversible — cells lose viability |
| Energy requirement | Often requires active metabolism to work | May act on dormant cells |
| Example in food | **Refrigeration of milk** (slows spoilage bacteria) | **Pasteurization of fruit juice** (kills pathogens) |

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module Choice Rule:** KTU allows students to answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B. Both questions test the same CO but from different analytical angles.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *(Choose this OR Question B)*

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Describe the various **physical methods** used for the control of microorganisms in foods. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **principles of Hurdle Technology**. Discuss any **five common hurdles** used in food preservation with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Physical Methods:**

1. **Heat Treatment (Thermal Methods):**
   - **Moist heat** is more effective than dry heat due to better heat transfer.
   - **Pasteurization** — HTST (High Temperature Short Time): **72 °C for 15 s** inactivates vegetative pathogens like *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*, *Coxiella burnetii*, *Salmonella*.
   - **UHT (Ultra High Temperature)** — 138 °C for 2 s for shelf-stable milk.
   - **Canning/Sterilization** — 121 °C, 15 psi for 15 min (F₀ ≥ 3 min) destroys *Clostridium botulinum* spores (12-D concept).

2. **Cold Preservation:**
   - **Refrigeration (0–7 °C)** inhibits mesophilic spoilage organisms but *Listeria monocytogenes* and *Pseudomonas* can still grow (psychrotrophs).
   - **Freezing (−18 °C)** stops microbial metabolism; some Gram-negative bacteria suffer *cold shock* injury but spores survive.

3. **Radiation:**
   - **Non-ionizing (UV-C 254 nm)** — surface disinfection only; induces thymine dimers.
   - **Ionizing (γ-rays 1–10 kGy)** — penetrates bulk food, breaks DNA strands; used for spices, meat, and poultry (cold pasteurization).

4. **Drying / Dehydration:**
   - Reduces **water activity (a<sub>w</sub>)** below the threshold for microbial growth (e.g., 0.60 for crackers).

5. **Mechanical Removal:**
   - **Membrane filtration (0.22 µm)** used for beer, wine, juices to remove yeasts and bacteria.

6. **Non-thermal Novel Methods:**
   - **HPP (600 MPa, 5 min)** — inactivates vegetative pathogens while retaining freshness.
   - **PEF, Ultrasound** — emerging technologies for juice and liquid foods.

**(b) Hurdle Technology:**

> **Principle** (Leistner & Gorris, 1995): A combination of *gentle preservation techniques* (hurdles) is applied simultaneously, so that microbes face **multiple stress factors** they cannot overcome collectively, even though each individual hurdle is mild.

**Five Common Hurdles with Examples:**

| # | Hurdle | Mechanism | Food Example |
|---|--------|-----------|---------------|
| 1 | Low pH | Disrupts enzyme activity, ATP generation | Yogurt, pickles |
| 2 | Reduced a<sub>w</sub> | Osmotic stress on cells | Jam, salted fish |
| 3 | Mild heat | Partial inactivation | Sous-vide meat |
| 4 | Refrigeration | Slows metabolism | Ready-to-eat meals |
| 5 | Nisin / preservatives | Membrane disruption | Processed cheese |

**Advantages of Hurdle Technology:**
- Retains sensory and nutritional quality
- Reduces energy costs
- Avoids over-reliance on a single extreme treatment
- Synergistic — microbial homeostasis is destabilized

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *(Alternative Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Define **D-value, Z-value, and F-value**. Establish the mathematical relationship between them. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A canned food processor wants to ensure a **12-log reduction** of *Clostridium botulinum* spores. If **D₁₂₁ = 0.25 min** at the process temperature of **121 °C**, calculate:
  - (i) The required **F₀** value
  - (ii) The **death rate constant k**
  - (iii) The survivors from an initial load of **10⁸ spores** after the process. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Definitions and Relationship:**

- **D-value (Decimal Reduction Time):** Time at a given temperature to inactivate **90 %** of the microbial population.

$$D = \dfrac{t}{\log_{10} N_0 - \log_{10} N}$$

- **Z-value:** The temperature rise required to reduce the D-value by a factor of 10.

$$Z = \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{\log_{10} D_1 - \log_{10} D_2}$$

- **F-value:** The equivalent time, in minutes, of thermal destruction at a reference temperature, integrating lethal effect across the entire heating profile.

$$F = D \times (\log_{10} N_0 - \log_{10} N)$$

**Mathematical Relationship:**

From the F-equation, we write:

$$F = D \cdot (\log_{10} N_0 - \log_{10} N)$$

If we substitute $\log_{10} N_0 - \log_{10} N = n$ (number of decimal reductions):

$$F = n \cdot D$$

At the reference temperature (121.1 °C, Z = 10 °C), this becomes:

$$\boxed{F_0 = 12 \times D_{121.1}}$$

This is the **12-D commercial sterilization** benchmark.

**(b) Numerical:**

**Given:** D₁₂₁ = 0.25 min, target reduction = 12 logs, N₀ = 10⁸ spores.

**(i) F₀ Calculation:**

$$F_0 = n \cdot D_{121} = 12 \times 0.25$$

$$\boxed{F_0 = 3.0 \text{ min}}$$

**(ii) Death Rate Constant k:**

$$k = \dfrac{2.303}{D} = \dfrac{2.303}{0.25}$$

$$\boxed{k = 9.212 \text{ min}^{-1}}$$

**(iii) Survivors Calculation:**

After 12-log reduction, survivors are reduced by a factor of 10¹².

$$N = \dfrac{N_0}{10^{12}} = \dfrac{10^{8}}{10^{12}} = 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 10^{-4} \text{ spores, i.e., effectively zero}}$$

This proves the safety of commercial canning against *C. botulinum*.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't mix up D and Z.** Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** if a student writes "D-value = temperature change" instead of "time for 1-log reduction at a fixed temperature."
> 2. **Always specify the temperature** when quoting D (e.g., write **D₇₂**, not just "D").
> 3. **Include units** in every numerical answer — `min`, `°C`, `kGy`, etc.
> 4. **Show the log reduction clearly** (write "12-log reduction") rather than vaguely stating "kill all the bacteria."
> 5. **Do not confuse F-value and F₀** — F is general; F₀ specifically refers to **121.1 °C** with Z = 10 °C.
> 6. In **Hurdle Technology** answers, *name at least 5 hurdles* and pair each with a real food example — generic answers lose marks.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- 🔹 **Control ≠ sterilization.** Three goals: **inhibit, inactivate, remove.**
- 🔹 **Three pillars:** Physical, Chemical, Biological.
- 🔹 **D-value** = time for 1-log kill at constant T (units: **minutes**).
- 🔹 **Z-value** = °C needed to reduce D by 10× (units: **°C**).
- 🔹 **F-value** = total lethal effect (units: **minutes**).
- 🔹 **F₀ = 12 × D₁₂₁** → commercial sterilization target for *C. botulinum*.
- 🔹 **k = 2.303 / D** (first-order death rate).
- 🔹 **Water activity thresholds:** bacteria stop < 0.86, yeasts < 0.70, molds < 0.60.
- 🔹 **Pasteurization ≠ sterilization.** Pasteurization kills *vegetative pathogens only*; sterilization also kills *spores*.
- 🔹 **HTST** = 72 °C / 15 s; **UHT** = 138 °C / 2 s; **Autoclave** = 121 °C / 15 min.
- 🔹 **Hurdle Technology** uses **multiple mild barriers** synergistically — concept by **Leistner & Gorris**.
- 🔹 **Nisin** is the only bacteriocin approved worldwide (GRAS).
- 🔹 **UV-C** acts *only on surfaces*; **γ-radiation** penetrates bulk foods.
- 🔹 **HPP** (600 MPa) inactivates vegetative pathogens but **NOT bacterial spores**.
- 🔹 Survivors after 12-D = $10^{N_0 - 12}$ — typically a fractional cell ⇒ safe.
- 🔹 **Bacteriostatic** is *reversible*; **Bactericidal** is *irreversible*.
